From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Country in Oceania

This article is about the country. For the continent, see [Australia (continent)](/wiki/Australia_(continent) "Australia (continent)"). For other uses, see [Australia (disambiguation)](/wiki/Australia_(disambiguation) "Australia (disambiguation)").

Not to be confused with [Australasia](/wiki/Australasia "Australasia"), [Austrasia](/wiki/Austrasia "Austrasia"), or [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria").

**Australia**, officially the **Commonwealth of Australia**, is a country comprising [the mainland](/wiki/Mainland_Australia "Mainland Australia") of the [Australian continent](/wiki/Australia_(continent) "Australia (continent)"), the island of [Tasmania](/wiki/Tasmania "Tasmania") and [numerous smaller islands](/wiki/List_of_islands_of_Australia "List of islands of Australia").[[N 6]](#cite_note-22) It has a total area of 7,688,287 km2 (2,968,464 sq mi), making it the [sixth-largest country in the world](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_dependencies_by_area "List of countries and dependencies by area") and the largest in [Oceania](/wiki/Oceania "Oceania"). Australia is the world's flattest and driest inhabited continent. It is a [megadiverse country](/wiki/Megadiverse_countries "Megadiverse countries"), and its size gives it a wide variety of landscapes and [climates](/wiki/Climate_of_Australia "Climate of Australia") including [deserts](/wiki/Deserts_of_Australia "Deserts of Australia") in the [interior](/wiki/Outback "Outback") and [tropical rainforests](/wiki/Forests_of_Australia "Forests of Australia") along the [coast](/wiki/Eastern_states_of_Australia "Eastern states of Australia").

The ancestors of [Aboriginal Australians](/wiki/Aboriginal_Australians "Aboriginal Australians") began arriving from [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia") 50,000 to 65,000 years ago, during the [Last Glacial Period](/wiki/Last_Glacial_Period "Last Glacial Period"). By the time of British settlement, Aboriginal Australians spoke more than 250 distinct languages and had one of the oldest living cultures in the world.[[17]](#cite_note-Flood_2019-2-23) [Australia's written history](/wiki/History_of_Australia "History of Australia") commenced with [Dutch exploration](/wiki/European_maritime_exploration_of_Australia "European maritime exploration of Australia") of most of the coastline in the 17th century. British colonisation began in 1788 with the establishment of the penal [colony of New South Wales](/wiki/Colony_of_New_South_Wales "Colony of New South Wales"). By the mid-19th century, most of the continent had been explored by European settlers and five additional self-governing [British colonies](/wiki/Crown_colony "Crown colony") were established, each gaining [responsible government](/wiki/Responsible_government "Responsible government") by 1890. The [colonies federated](/wiki/Federation_of_Australia "Federation of Australia") in 1901, forming the Commonwealth of Australia. This continued a process of increasing autonomy from the United Kingdom, highlighted by the *[Statute of Westminster Adoption Act 1942](/wiki/Statute_of_Westminster_Adoption_Act_1942 "Statute of Westminster Adoption Act 1942")*, and culminating in the [Australia Acts](/wiki/Australia_Acts "Australia Acts") of 1986.

Australia is a [constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy") with a [parliamentary democracy](/wiki/Parliamentary_democracy "Parliamentary democracy"), and a [federation](/wiki/Federation "Federation") comprising [six states and ten territories](/wiki/States_and_territories_of_Australia "States and territories of Australia"). Its population of almost 28 million is highly urbanised and heavily concentrated on the eastern seaboard.[[18]](#cite_note-Australian_Bureau_of_Statistics-2023-24) [Canberra](/wiki/Canberra "Canberra") is the nation's capital, while [its most populous cities](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Australia_by_population "List of cities in Australia by population") are [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney") and [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne "Melbourne"), each with a population of more than five million. [Australia's culture](/wiki/Culture_of_Australia "Culture of Australia") is diverse, and the country has one of the [highest foreign-born populations in the world](/wiki/List_of_sovereign_states_by_immigrant_and_emigrant_population "List of sovereign states by immigrant and emigrant population"). It has a highly [developed economy](/wiki/Developed_economy "Developed economy") and one of the [highest per capita incomes globally](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GNI_(PPP)_per_capita "List of countries by GNI (PPP) per capita"). Its abundant natural resources and well-developed [international trade relations](/wiki/List_of_the_largest_trading_partners_of_Australia "List of the largest trading partners of Australia") are crucial to [the country's economy](/wiki/Economy_of_Australia "Economy of Australia"). It [ranks highly](/wiki/International_rankings_of_Australia "International rankings of Australia") for quality of life, health, education, economic freedom, civil liberties and political rights.

Australia is a [middle power](/wiki/Middle_power "Middle power"), and has the world's [thirteenth-highest military expenditure](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_military_expenditures "List of countries by military expenditures"). It is a member of international groups including: the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations"), the [G20](/wiki/G20 "G20"), the [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD"), the [World Trade Organization](/wiki/World_Trade_Organization "World Trade Organization"), [Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation](/wiki/Asia-Pacific_Economic_Cooperation "Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation"), the [Pacific Islands Forum](/wiki/Pacific_Islands_Forum "Pacific Islands Forum"), the [Pacific Community](/wiki/Pacific_Community "Pacific Community") and the [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations"). Australia also participates in the defence, intelligence and security alliances [ANZUS](/wiki/ANZUS "ANZUS"), [AUKUS](/wiki/AUKUS "AUKUS"), and [Five Eyes](/wiki/Five_Eyes "Five Eyes"). It is a [major non-NATO ally](/wiki/Major_non-NATO_ally "Major non-NATO ally") of the United States.

## Etymology

Main article: [Name of Australia](/wiki/Name_of_Australia "Name of Australia")

The name *Australia* (pronounced [/əˈstreɪliə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") in [Australian English](/wiki/Australian_English "Australian English"))[[19]](#cite_note-25) is derived from the Latin *[Terra Australis Incognita](/wiki/Terra_Australis_Incognita "Terra Australis Incognita")* ('unknown southern land'), a name used for a hypothetical continent in the Southern Hemisphere since ancient times.[[20]](#cite_note-National_Library_of_Australia-2024-26) Several 16th-century cartographers used the word *Australia* on maps, but not to identify modern Australia.[[21]](#cite_note-27)

When the Dutch began visiting and mapping Australia in the 17th century, they called the continent [*New Holland*](/wiki/New_Holland_(Australia) "New Holland (Australia)"). The name *Australia* was popularised by the explorer [Matthew Flinders](/wiki/Matthew_Flinders "Matthew Flinders"), who circumnavigated the continent in 1803. However, when his account of his voyage was published in 1814, the name *Terra Australis* was used.[[20]](#cite_note-National_Library_of_Australia-2024-26)

Governor of New South Wales [Lachlan Macquarie](/wiki/Lachlan_Macquarie "Lachlan Macquarie") officially recommended the name *Australia* to replace *New Holland* in December 1817. The [British Admiralty](/wiki/Admiralty_(United_Kingdom) "Admiralty (United Kingdom)") adopted the name in 1824, and the [British Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom") used it in legislation in 1828.[[20]](#cite_note-National_Library_of_Australia-2024-26) The United Kingdom [Hydrographic Office](/wiki/United_Kingdom_Hydrographic_Office "United Kingdom Hydrographic Office") used the new name in *The Australia Directory* of 1830.[[22]](#cite_note-28) The name "[Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth "Commonwealth") of Australia" for the new federation of the six former colonies was formalised in the *[Commonwealth of Australia Constitution Act 1900](/wiki/Constitution_of_Australia "Constitution of Australia")* (UK).[[20]](#cite_note-National_Library_of_Australia-2024-26)

Colloquial names for Australia include "Oz", "Straya" and "[Down Under](/wiki/Down_Under "Down Under")".[[23]](#cite_note-29)

## History

Main article: [History of Australia](/wiki/History_of_Australia "History of Australia")

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of Australian history](/wiki/Timeline_of_Australian_history "Timeline of Australian history").

### Indigenous prehistory

Main articles: [Prehistory of Australia](/wiki/Prehistory_of_Australia "Prehistory of Australia") and [Indigenous Australians](/wiki/Indigenous_Australians "Indigenous Australians")

[Indigenous Australians](/wiki/Indigenous_Australians "Indigenous Australians") comprise two broad groups:

* [Aboriginal Australians](/wiki/Aboriginal_Australians "Aboriginal Australians"), who are the various [Indigenous peoples](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples "Indigenous peoples") of the Australian mainland and many of its islands, including [Tasmania](/wiki/Aboriginal_Tasmanians "Aboriginal Tasmanians")
* [Torres Strait Islanders](/wiki/Torres_Strait_Islanders "Torres Strait Islanders"), who are a distinct [Melanesian](/wiki/Melanesia "Melanesia") people of [Torres Strait Islands](/wiki/Torres_Strait_Islands "Torres Strait Islands")

Human habitation of the Australian continent is estimated to have begun 50,000 to 65,000 years ago,[[24]](#cite_note-Veth-30)[[25]](#cite_note-Clarkson-2017-31)[[26]](#cite_note-Williams-2021-32)[[27]](#cite_note-33) with the migration of people by [land bridges](/wiki/Land_bridge "Land bridge") and short sea crossings from what is now Southeast Asia.[[24]](#cite_note-Veth-30) It is uncertain how many waves of immigration may have contributed to these ancestors of modern Aboriginal Australians.[[28]](#cite_note-Malaspinas-2016-34)[[29]](#cite_note-35) The [Madjedbebe](/wiki/Madjedbebe "Madjedbebe") rock shelter in [Arnhem Land](/wiki/Arnhem_Land "Arnhem Land") is possibly the oldest site showing the presence of humans in Australia.[[25]](#cite_note-Clarkson-2017-31)[[26]](#cite_note-Williams-2021-32)[[30]](#cite_note-36) The oldest human remains found are the [Lake Mungo remains](/wiki/Lake_Mungo_remains "Lake Mungo remains"), which have been dated to approximately 42,000 years ago.[[31]](#cite_note-37)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Bradshaw_rock_paintings.jpg/250px-Bradshaw_rock_paintings.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bradshaw_rock_paintings.jpg)

[Aboriginal rock art](/wiki/Aboriginal_rock_art "Aboriginal rock art") in the [Kimberley](/wiki/Kimberley_(Western_Australia) "Kimberley (Western Australia)") region of Western Australia

Aboriginal Australian culture is one of the oldest continuous cultures on Earth.[[32]](#cite_note-38)[[33]](#cite_note-39)[[34]](#cite_note-40)[[28]](#cite_note-Malaspinas-2016-34) At the time of first European contact, Aboriginal Australians belonged to wide range of societies, with diverse economies spread across at least [250 different language groups](/wiki/Australian_Aboriginal_languages "Australian Aboriginal languages").[[35]](#cite_note-41)[[36]](#cite_note-42)[[17]](#cite_note-Flood_2019-2-23) Estimates of the Aboriginal population before British settlement range from 300,000 to 3 million.[[37]](#cite_note-43) Aboriginal Australians cultures were (and remain) deeply connected with the land and the environment, with stories of [The Dreaming](/wiki/The_Dreaming "The Dreaming") maintained through [oral tradition](/wiki/Oral_tradition "Oral tradition"), [songs](/wiki/Songline "Songline"), dance and paintings.[[38]](#cite_note-Mawson-2021-44) Certain groups engaged in [fire-stick farming](/wiki/Fire-stick_farming "Fire-stick farming"),[[39]](#cite_note-45)[[40]](#cite_note-46) [fish farming](/wiki/Fish_farming "Fish farming"),[[41]](#cite_note-47)[[42]](#cite_note-48) and built [semi-permanent shelters](/wiki/Indigenous_architecture#Australia "Indigenous architecture").[[43]](#cite_note-49) These practices have variously been characterised as "[hunter-gatherer](/wiki/Hunter-gatherer "Hunter-gatherer")", "[agricultural](/wiki/Agriculture "Agriculture")", "natural cultivation" and "intensification".[[38]](#cite_note-Mawson-2021-44)[[44]](#cite_note-50)[[45]](#cite_note-51)[[46]](#cite_note-Flood_2019-52)

Torres Strait Islander people first settled their islands at least 2,500 years ago.[[47]](#cite_note-Veth-2-53)[[48]](#cite_note-54) Culturally and linguistically distinct from mainland Aboriginal peoples, they were seafarers and obtained their livelihood from seasonal horticulture and the resources of their reefs and seas. Agriculture also developed on some islands and villages appeared by the 1300s.[[47]](#cite_note-Veth-2-53) By the mid-18th century in northern Australia, [contact, trade and cross-cultural engagement](/wiki/Makassan_contact_with_Australia "Makassan contact with Australia") had been established between local Aboriginal groups and [Makassan](/wiki/Makassar_people "Makassar people") [trepangers](/wiki/Trepanging "Trepanging"), visiting from present-day Indonesia.[[49]](#cite_note-55)[[50]](#cite_note-56)[[51]](#cite_note-57)

### European exploration and colonisation

Main articles: [European maritime exploration of Australia](/wiki/European_maritime_exploration_of_Australia "European maritime exploration of Australia"), [European land exploration of Australia](/wiki/European_land_exploration_of_Australia "European land exploration of Australia"), and [History of Australia (1788–1850)](/wiki/History_of_Australia_(1788%E2%80%931850) "History of Australia (1788–1850)")

[![Landing of Lieutenant James Cook at Botany Bay, 29 April 1770](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Landing_of_Lieutenant_James_Cook_at_Botany_Bay%2C_29_April_1770_%28painting_by_E_Phillips_Fox%29.jpg/250px-Landing_of_Lieutenant_James_Cook_at_Botany_Bay%2C_29_April_1770_%28painting_by_E_Phillips_Fox%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Landing_of_Lieutenant_James_Cook_at_Botany_Bay,_29_April_1770_(painting_by_E_Phillips_Fox).jpg)

Landing of [James Cook](/wiki/James_Cook "James Cook") at [Botany Bay](/wiki/Botany_Bay "Botany Bay") on 29 April 1770

The [Dutch East India Company](/wiki/Dutch_East_India_Company "Dutch East India Company") ship, *[Duyfken](/wiki/Duyfken "Duyfken")*, captained by [Willem Janszoon](/wiki/Willem_Janszoon "Willem Janszoon"), made the first documented European landing in Australia in 1606.[[52]](#cite_note-58) Later that year, [Luís Vaz de Torres](/wiki/Lu%C3%ADs_Vaz_de_Torres "Luís Vaz de Torres") sailed to the north of Australia through [Torres Strait](/wiki/Torres_Strait "Torres Strait"), along New Guinea's southern coast.[[53]](#cite_note-59) [Abel Tasman](/wiki/Abel_Tasman "Abel Tasman")'s voyage of 1642 was the first known European expedition to reach [Van Diemen's Land](/wiki/Van_Diemen%27s_Land "Van Diemen's Land"). On his second voyage of 1644, he mapped the north coast of Australia south of New Guinea. Following Tasman's voyages, the Dutch were able to make almost complete maps of Australia's northern and western coasts and much of its southern and south-eastern Tasmanian coasts. They named the continent [New Holland](/wiki/New_Holland_(Australia) "New Holland (Australia)").[[54]](#cite_note-60)

In 1770, Captain [James Cook](/wiki/James_Cook "James Cook") sailed along and mapped the east coast, which he named "[New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales")" and claimed for Great Britain.[[55]](#cite_note-61) in 1786, the British government announced its intention to establish a [penal colony](/wiki/Penal_colony "Penal colony") in New South Wales.[[56]](#cite_note-62) On 26 January 1788, the [First Fleet](/wiki/First_Fleet "First Fleet") commanded by Captain [Arthur Phillip](/wiki/Arthur_Phillip "Arthur Phillip"), arrived at [Sydney Cove](/wiki/Sydney_Cove "Sydney Cove"), Port Jackson. A camp was established and the [Union Flag](/wiki/Flag_of_Great_Britain "Flag of Great Britain") raised. The date later became [Australia's national day](/wiki/Australia_Day "Australia Day").[[57]](#cite_note-63)

Most early settlers were [convicts](/wiki/Convicts_in_Australia "Convicts in Australia"), [transported](/wiki/Penal_transportation "Penal transportation") for petty crimes and [assigned](/wiki/Convict_assignment "Convict assignment") as labourers or servants to "free settlers" (willing immigrants). Once [emancipated](/wiki/Emancipist "Emancipist"), convicts tended to integrate into colonial society. Aboriginal resistance, convict rebellions and [bushranging](/wiki/Bushranger "Bushranger") were sometimes suppressed under martial law.[[58]](#cite_note-64)[[59]](#cite_note-65) The 1808 [Rum Rebellion](/wiki/Rum_Rebellion "Rum Rebellion"), carried out by officers of the [New South Wales Corp](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Corps "New South Wales Corps"), led to a temporary [military junta](/wiki/Military_junta "Military junta").[[60]](#cite_note-66) During the next two decades, social and economic reforms, together with the establishment of a [Legislative Council](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Legislative_Council "New South Wales Legislative Council") and [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_New_South_Wales "Supreme Court of New South Wales"), saw the penal colony transition to a civil society.[[61]](#cite_note-67)

The indigenous population declined for 150 years following European settlement, mainly due to infectious disease.[[62]](#cite_note-68) British colonial authorities did not sign any treaties with [Aboriginal groups](/wiki/Aboriginal_Australians#Groups_and_sub-groups "Aboriginal Australians").[[63]](#cite_note-69)[[64]](#cite_note-70) As settlement expanded, tens of thousands of Indigenous people and thousands of settlers were killed in [frontier conflicts](/wiki/Australian_frontier_wars "Australian frontier wars"), which many historians argue included acts of [genocide](/wiki/Genocide_of_Indigenous_Australians "Genocide of Indigenous Australians") by settlers.[[65]](#cite_note-Reynolds_2022-71)[[66]](#cite_note-72) Settlers dispossessed surviving Indigenous peoples of most of their land.[[65]](#cite_note-Reynolds_2022-71)

### Colonial expansion

Main articles: [History of Australia (1788–1850)](/wiki/History_of_Australia_(1788%E2%80%931850) "History of Australia (1788–1850)") and [History of Australia (1851–1900)](/wiki/History_of_Australia_(1851%E2%80%931900) "History of Australia (1851–1900)")

[![A calm body of water is in the foreground. The shoreline is about 200 metres away. To the left, close to the shore, are three tall gum trees; behind them on an incline are ruins, including walls and watchtowers of light-coloured stone and brick, what appear to be the foundations of walls, and grassed areas. To the right lie the outer walls of a large rectangular four-storey building dotted with regularly spaced windows. Forested land rises gently to a peak several kilometres back from the shore.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/PortArthurPenitentiary.jpg/250px-PortArthurPenitentiary.jpg)](/wiki/File:PortArthurPenitentiary.jpg)

Tasmania's [Port Arthur](/wiki/Port_Arthur,_Tasmania "Port Arthur, Tasmania") penal settlement is one of eleven UNESCO World Heritage-listed [Australian Convict Sites](/wiki/Australian_Convict_Sites "Australian Convict Sites").

In 1803, a settlement was established in [Van Diemen's Land](/wiki/Van_Diemen%27s_Land "Van Diemen's Land") (present-day [Tasmania](/wiki/Tasmania "Tasmania")),[[67]](#cite_note-73) and in 1813, [Gregory Blaxland](/wiki/Gregory_Blaxland "Gregory Blaxland"), [William Lawson](/wiki/William_Lawson_(explorer) "William Lawson (explorer)") and [William Wentworth](/wiki/William_Wentworth "William Wentworth") [crossed the Blue Mountains](/wiki/1813_crossing_of_the_Blue_Mountains "1813 crossing of the Blue Mountains") west of Sydney, opening the interior to European settlement.[[68]](#cite_note-74) The British claim was extended to the whole Australian continent in 1827 when Major [Edmund Lockyer](/wiki/Edmund_Lockyer "Edmund Lockyer") established a settlement on [King George Sound](/wiki/King_George_Sound "King George Sound") (modern-day [Albany](/wiki/Albany,_Western_Australia "Albany, Western Australia")).[[69]](#cite_note-75) The [Swan River Colony](/wiki/Swan_River_Colony "Swan River Colony") (present-day [Perth](/wiki/Perth "Perth")) was established in 1829, evolving into the largest Australian colony by area, [Western Australia](/wiki/History_of_Western_Australia "History of Western Australia").[[70]](#cite_note-76) Separate colonies were carved from New South Wales: Tasmania in 1825, [South Australia](/wiki/British_colonisation_of_South_Australia "British colonisation of South Australia") in 1836, [Victoria](/wiki/Colony_of_Victoria "Colony of Victoria") in 1851, and [Queensland](/wiki/Colony_of_Queensland "Colony of Queensland") in 1859.[[71]](#cite_note-77) South Australia and Victoria were founded as free colonies – they never accepted transported convicts.[[72]](#cite_note-78) Growing [opposition to the convict system](/wiki/Australasian_Anti-Transportation_League "Australasian Anti-Transportation League") culminated in its abolition in the eastern colonies by the 1850s. Initially a free colony, Western Australia accepted convicts from 1850 to 1868.[[73]](#cite_note-79)

The six colonies individually gained [responsible government](/wiki/Responsible_government "Responsible government") between 1855 and 1890, managing most of their own affairs while remaining part of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire").[[74]](#cite_note-80) The Colonial Office in London retained control of some matters, notably foreign affairs.[[75]](#cite_note-81) The colonial parliaments progressively extended voting rights to adult men from 1856, with [women's suffrage](/wiki/Women%27s_suffrage "Women's suffrage") on equal terms following between the 1890s and 1900s. Some colonies introduced racial restrictions on voting from 1885.[[76]](#cite_note-82)

In the mid-19th century, explorers such as [Burke and Wills](/wiki/Burke_and_Wills_expedition "Burke and Wills expedition") charted Australia's interior.[[77]](#cite_note-83) A [series of gold rushes](/wiki/Australian_gold_rushes "Australian gold rushes") beginning in the early 1850s led to an influx of new migrants from [China](/wiki/Chinese_Australians "Chinese Australians"), North America and continental Europe,[[78]](#cite_note-84) as well as outbreaks of [bushranging](/wiki/Bushranger "Bushranger") and civil unrest; the latter peaked in 1854 when [Ballarat](/wiki/Ballarat "Ballarat") miners launched the [Eureka Rebellion](/wiki/Eureka_Rebellion "Eureka Rebellion") against gold licence fees.[[79]](#cite_note-85) The 1860s saw the rise of [blackbirding](/wiki/Blackbirding "Blackbirding"), where [South Sea Islanders](/wiki/South_Sea_Islanders "South Sea Islanders") were coerced or abducted into [indentured labour](/wiki/Indentured_labour "Indentured labour"), mainly by Queensland colonists.[[80]](#cite_note-86)[[81]](#cite_note-87)

From 1886, Australian colonial governments began [removing many Aboriginal children](/wiki/Stolen_Generations "Stolen Generations") from their families and communities, justified on the grounds of child protection and [forced assimilation](/wiki/Forced_assimilation "Forced assimilation") policies.[[82]](#cite_note-88)[[83]](#cite_note-89)[[84]](#cite_note-90) The [Second Boer War](/wiki/Second_Boer_War "Second Boer War") (1899–1902) marked the largest overseas deployment of [Australia's colonial forces](/wiki/Colonial_forces_of_Australia "Colonial forces of Australia").[[85]](#cite_note-91)[[86]](#cite_note-92)

### Federation to the World Wars

Main article: [History of Australia (1901–1945)](/wiki/History_of_Australia_(1901%E2%80%931945) "History of Australia (1901–1945)")

See also: [Federation of Australia](/wiki/Federation_of_Australia "Federation of Australia"), [Military history of Australia during World War I](/wiki/Military_history_of_Australia_during_World_War_I "Military history of Australia during World War I"), and [Military history of Australia during World War II](/wiki/Military_history_of_Australia_during_World_War_II "Military history of Australia during World War II")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/Opening_of_the_first_parliament.jpg/250px-Opening_of_the_first_parliament.jpg)](/wiki/File:Opening_of_the_first_parliament.jpg)

*[The Big Picture](/wiki/The_Big_Picture_(painting) "The Big Picture (painting)")*, a painting by [Tom Roberts](/wiki/Tom_Roberts "Tom Roberts"), depicts the opening of the first Australian Parliament in 1901.

On 1 January 1901, [federation of the colonies](/wiki/Federation_of_Australia "Federation of Australia") was achieved after a decade of planning, [constitutional conventions](/wiki/Constitutional_Convention_(Australia) "Constitutional Convention (Australia)") and [referendums](/wiki/1898%E2%80%931900_Australian_constitutional_referendums "1898–1900 Australian constitutional referendums"), resulting in the establishment of the Commonwealth of Australia as a nation under the new [Australian Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Australia "Constitution of Australia").[[87]](#cite_note-93)

From 1901, Australia was a self-governing [dominion](/wiki/Dominion "Dominion") within the British Empire.[[88]](#cite_note-94) It was one of the founding members of the [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations") in 1920,[[89]](#cite_note-95) and the [United Nations](/wiki/Member_states_of_the_United_Nations "Member states of the United Nations") in 1945.[[90]](#cite_note-96) The *[Statute of Westminster 1931](/wiki/Statute_of_Westminster_1931 "Statute of Westminster 1931")* ended the ability of the UK to legislate for Australia at the federal level without Australia's consent. Australia [adopted it](/wiki/Statute_of_Westminster_Adoption_Act_1942 "Statute of Westminster Adoption Act 1942") in 1942, but it was backdated to 1939 to confirm the validity of legislation passed during World War II.[[91]](#cite_note-97)

The [Australian Capital Territory](/wiki/Australian_Capital_Territory "Australian Capital Territory") was formed in 1911 as the location for the future federal capital of [Canberra](/wiki/Canberra "Canberra"). While it was being constructed, [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne "Melbourne") served as the temporary capital from 1901 to 1927.[[92]](#cite_note-98) The [Northern Territory](/wiki/Northern_Territory "Northern Territory") was transferred from the control of South Australia to the Commonwealth in 1911.[[93]](#cite_note-99) Australia took over the administration of the [Territory of Papua](/wiki/Territory_of_Papua "Territory of Papua") (which had previously been a British colony) in 1905 and of the [Territory of New Guinea](/wiki/Territory_of_New_Guinea "Territory of New Guinea") (formerly [German New Guinea](/wiki/German_New_Guinea "German New Guinea")) in 1920. The two were unified as the [Territory of Papua and New Guinea](/wiki/Territory_of_Papua_and_New_Guinea "Territory of Papua and New Guinea") in 1949 and gained independence from Australia in 1975.[[94]](#cite_note-100)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/Darwin_42.jpg/250px-Darwin_42.jpg)](/wiki/File:Darwin_42.jpg)

The 1942 [Bombing of Darwin](/wiki/Bombing_of_Darwin "Bombing of Darwin"), the first of more than 100 [Japanese air raids on Australia](/wiki/Air_raids_on_Australia,_1942%E2%80%931943 "Air raids on Australia, 1942–1943") during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II")

In 1914, Australia joined the [Allies](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_I "Allies of World War I") in the First World War, and took part in the fighting on several fronts.[[95]](#cite_note-101) Of the 324,000 men who served overseas, about 60,000 were killed and another 152,000 were wounded.[[96]](#cite_note-102) Many Australians regard the defeat of the [Australian and New Zealand Army Corps](/wiki/Australian_and_New_Zealand_Army_Corps "Australian and New Zealand Army Corps") (ANZAC) at [Gallipoli](/wiki/Gallipoli_Campaign "Gallipoli Campaign") in 1915 as the "baptism of fire" that forged the [new nation's identity](/wiki/Anzac_spirit "Anzac spirit").[[97]](#cite_note-Macintyre_2020-103)[[98]](#cite_note-104) The [beginning of the campaign](/wiki/Landing_at_Anzac_Cove "Landing at Anzac Cove") is commemorated annually on [Anzac Day](/wiki/Anzac_Day "Anzac Day"), a date which rivals [Australia Day](/wiki/Australia_Day "Australia Day") as the nation's most important.[[97]](#cite_note-Macintyre_2020-103)[[99]](#cite_note-105)

From 1939 to 1945, Australia joined the [Allies](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II") in fighting the Second World War. Australia's [armed forces](/wiki/Australian_Defence_Force "Australian Defence Force") fought in the [Pacific](/wiki/Pacific_War "Pacific War"), [European](/wiki/European_Theatre_of_World_War_II "European Theatre of World War II") and [Mediterranean and Middle East](/wiki/Mediterranean_and_Middle_East_theatre_of_World_War_II "Mediterranean and Middle East theatre of World War II") [theatres](/wiki/List_of_theaters_and_campaigns_of_World_War_II "List of theaters and campaigns of World War II").[[100]](#cite_note-106) The shock of Britain's [defeat in Singapore](/wiki/Fall_of_Singapore "Fall of Singapore") in 1942, followed soon after by the [bombing of Darwin](/wiki/Bombing_of_Darwin "Bombing of Darwin") and [other Japanese attacks on Australian soil](/wiki/Air_raids_on_Australia,_1942%E2%80%9343 "Air raids on Australia, 1942–43"), led to a widespread belief in Australia that [a Japanese invasion was imminent](/wiki/Proposed_Japanese_invasion_of_Australia_during_World_War_II "Proposed Japanese invasion of Australia during World War II"), and a shift from the United Kingdom to the [United States](/wiki/Australia%E2%80%93United_States_relations "Australia–United States relations") as Australia's principal ally and security partner.[[101]](#cite_note-107) Since 1951, Australia has been allied with the United States under the [ANZUS](/wiki/ANZUS "ANZUS") treaty.[[102]](#cite_note-108)

### Post-war and contemporary eras

Main article: [History of Australia (1945–present)](/wiki/History_of_Australia_(1945%E2%80%93present) "History of Australia (1945–present)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/Dutch_Migrant_1954_MariaScholte%3D50000thToAustraliaPostWW2.jpg/250px-Dutch_Migrant_1954_MariaScholte%3D50000thToAustraliaPostWW2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dutch_Migrant_1954_MariaScholte%3D50000thToAustraliaPostWW2.jpg)

[Postwar migrants](/wiki/Post-war_immigration_to_Australia "Post-war immigration to Australia") from Europe arriving in Australia in 1954

In the three decades following World War II, Australia experienced significant increases in living standards, leisure time and suburban development.[[103]](#cite_note-109) Governments encouraged a [large wave of immigration from across Europe](/wiki/Post-war_immigration_to_Australia "Post-war immigration to Australia") and called these migrants "[New Australians](/wiki/New_Australians "New Australians")". High immigration was justified to Australians using the slogan "populate or perish",[[104]](#cite_note-110) and from the 1960s the [white Australia policy](/wiki/White_Australia_policy "White Australia policy") was gradually relaxed.[[105]](#cite_note-111)

A member of the [Western Bloc](/wiki/Western_Bloc "Western Bloc") during the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War"), Australia participated in the [Korean War](/wiki/Australia_in_the_Korean_War "Australia in the Korean War") and the [Malayan Emergency](/wiki/Military_history_of_Australia_during_the_Malayan_Emergency "Military history of Australia during the Malayan Emergency") during the 1950s and the [Vietnam War](/wiki/Military_history_of_Australia_during_the_Vietnam_War "Military history of Australia during the Vietnam War") from 1962 to 1973.[[106]](#cite_note-112)[[107]](#cite_note-113) Tensions over communist influence in society led to [unsuccessful attempts](/wiki/1951_Australian_Communist_Party_ban_referendum "1951 Australian Communist Party ban referendum") by the [Menzies Government](/wiki/Menzies_Government_(1949%E2%80%931966) "Menzies Government (1949–1966)") to ban the [Communist Party of Australia](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Australia "Communist Party of Australia"), and a [bitter split](/wiki/Australian_Labor_Party_split_of_1955 "Australian Labor Party split of 1955") in the [Labor Party](/wiki/Australian_Labor_Party "Australian Labor Party") in 1955.[[108]](#cite_note-114)

As a result of a [1967 referendum](/wiki/Australian_referendum,_1967_(Aboriginals) "Australian referendum, 1967 (Aboriginals)"), the federal government gained the power to legislate with regard to Aboriginal Australians, and Aboriginal Australians were fully included in the [census](/wiki/Census_in_Australia "Census in Australia").[[109]](#cite_note-115) [Pre-colonial land interests](/wiki/Aboriginal_title "Aboriginal title") (referred to as [native title](/wiki/Native_title_in_Australia "Native title in Australia") in Australia) was recognised in law for the first time when the [High Court of Australia](/wiki/High_Court_of_Australia "High Court of Australia") held in *[Mabo v Queensland (No 2)](/wiki/Mabo_v_Queensland_(No_2) "Mabo v Queensland (No 2)")* that Australia was not *[terra nullius](/wiki/Terra_nullius "Terra nullius")* ('land belonging to no one') at the time of European settlement.[[110]](#cite_note-116)[[111]](#cite_note-117)[[112]](#cite_note-118)

Following the abolition of the last vestiges of the [White Australia policy](/wiki/White_Australia_policy "White Australia policy") in 1973,[[113]](#cite_note-119) Australia's demography and culture transformed as a result of a large and ongoing wave of non-European immigration, mostly from Asia.[[114]](#cite_note-120) The late 20th century also saw an increasing focus on foreign policy ties with other [Asia–Pacific](/wiki/Asia%E2%80%93Pacific "Asia–Pacific") nations.[[115]](#cite_note-121) The [Australia Acts](/wiki/Australia_Acts "Australia Acts") of 1986 severed the remaining constitutional ties between Australia and the United Kingdom while maintaining the monarch in her independent capacity as [Queen of Australia](/wiki/Queen_of_Australia "Queen of Australia").[[116]](#cite_note-122)[[117]](#cite_note-123) In a [1999 constitutional referendum](/wiki/1999_Australian_republic_referendum "1999 Australian republic referendum"), 55% of voters rejected [abolishing the monarchy](/wiki/Republicanism_in_Australia "Republicanism in Australia") and becoming a republic.[[118]](#cite_note-124)

Following the [September 11 attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks") on the United States, Australia joined the United States in fighting the [Afghanistan War](/wiki/Military_history_of_Australia_during_the_War_in_Afghanistan "Military history of Australia during the War in Afghanistan") from 2001 to 2021 and the [Iraq War](/wiki/Australian_contribution_to_the_2003_invasion_of_Iraq "Australian contribution to the 2003 invasion of Iraq") from 2003 to 2009.[[119]](#cite_note-125) The nation's trade relations also became increasingly oriented towards East Asia in the 21st century, with China becoming the nation's [largest trading partner](/wiki/List_of_the_largest_trading_partners_of_Australia "List of the largest trading partners of Australia") by a large margin.[[120]](#cite_note-126)

In response to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Australia "COVID-19 pandemic in Australia"), from March 2020 [lockdowns](/wiki/COVID-19_lockdowns "COVID-19 lockdowns") and other restrictions on public gatherings and movement across the national and state borders were implemented by the Federal, state and territory governments. Following the rollout of vaccines in 2021, these restrictions were gradually eased. In October 2023, Australia declared that COVID-19 was no longer a communicable disease incident of national significance.[[121]](#cite_note-127)

## Geography

Main articles: [Geography of Australia](/wiki/Geography_of_Australia "Geography of Australia") and [Environment of Australia](/wiki/Environment_of_Australia "Environment of Australia")

See also: [Environmental issues in Australia](/wiki/Environmental_issues_in_Australia "Environmental issues in Australia")

### General characteristics

[![Map showing the topography of Australia, showing some elevation in the west and very high elevation in mountains in the south-east](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/Reliefmap_of_Australia.png/330px-Reliefmap_of_Australia.png)](/wiki/File:Reliefmap_of_Australia.png)

Topographic map of Australia. Dark green represents the lowest elevation and dark brown the highest

Australia consists of the mainland Australian continent, the island of Tasmania, numerous smaller offshore islands, and the remote offshore territories of [Ashmore and Cartier Islands](/wiki/Ashmore_and_Cartier_Islands "Ashmore and Cartier Islands"), [Christmas Island](/wiki/Christmas_Island "Christmas Island"), [Cocos (Keeling) Islands](/wiki/Cocos_(Keeling)_Islands "Cocos (Keeling) Islands"), [Coral Sea Islands](/wiki/Coral_Sea_Islands "Coral Sea Islands"), [Heard and McDonald Islands](/wiki/Heard_Island_and_McDonald_Islands "Heard Island and McDonald Islands"), and [Norfolk Island](/wiki/Norfolk_Island "Norfolk Island").[[122]](#cite_note-128) Australia also claims about 42% of Antarctica as the [Australian Antarctic Territory](/wiki/Australian_Antarctic_Territory "Australian Antarctic Territory"), but this claim is only recognised by four other countries.[[16]](#cite_note-Scott_2021-21)

Mainland Australia lies between latitudes [9°](/wiki/9th_parallel_south "9th parallel south") and [44° south](/wiki/44th_parallel_south "44th parallel south"), and longitudes [112°](/wiki/112th_meridian_east "112th meridian east") and [154° east](/wiki/154th_meridian_east "154th meridian east").[[8]](#cite_note-Geoscience_Australia-2014a-12) Surrounded by the Indian and Pacific oceans, Australia is separated from Asia by the [Arafura](/wiki/Arafura_Sea "Arafura Sea") and [Timor](/wiki/Timor_Sea "Timor Sea") seas, with the [Coral Sea](/wiki/Coral_Sea "Coral Sea") lying off the Queensland coast, and the [Tasman Sea](/wiki/Tasman_Sea "Tasman Sea") lying between Australia and New Zealand. The [Great Barrier Reef](/wiki/Great_Barrier_Reef "Great Barrier Reef"), the world's largest coral reef, lies a short distance off the north-east coast and extends for more than 2,300 km (1,400 mi).[[123]](#cite_note-129)

The mainland is the world's smallest continent and the country is the [sixth-largest by total area](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_outlying_territories_by_total_area "List of countries and outlying territories by total area").[[124]](#cite_note-Blewett_2012-130) Australia is sometimes considered the world's [largest island](/wiki/List_of_islands_by_area "List of islands by area")[[125]](#cite_note-131) and is often dubbed the "island continent".[[126]](#cite_note-132) It has 35,877 km (22,293 mi) of coastline (excluding all offshore islands),[[124]](#cite_note-Blewett_2012-130) and claims an [exclusive economic zone](/wiki/Exclusive_economic_zone_of_Australia "Exclusive economic zone of Australia") of 8,148,250 square kilometres (3,146,060 sq mi). This exclusive economic zone does not include the [Australian Antarctic Territory](/wiki/Australian_Antarctic_Territory "Australian Antarctic Territory").[[127]](#cite_note-133)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Fitzroy_Island.jpg/250px-Fitzroy_Island.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fitzroy_Island.jpg)

[Fitzroy Island](/wiki/Fitzroy_Island_(Queensland) "Fitzroy Island (Queensland)"), one of the 600 islands within the main archipelago of the [Great Barrier Reef](/wiki/Great_Barrier_Reef "Great Barrier Reef")

Most of Australia is arid or semi-arid.[[128]](#cite_note-Blewett_2012-3-134) In 2021, Australia had 10% of the global permanent meadows and pastureland.[[129]](#cite_note-135) [Forest cover](/wiki/Forest_cover "Forest cover") is around 17% of Australia's land area.[[130]](#cite_note-136)[[131]](#cite_note-137) The Australian mainland is relatively flat, with an average height of 325 metres (1,066 ft) compared with 870 metres (2,850 ft) for all continents.[[132]](#cite_note-138) The [Great Dividing Range](/wiki/Great_Dividing_Range "Great Dividing Range") runs along most of eastern Australia, dividing the central lowlands from the eastern highlands.[[133]](#cite_note-Geoscience_Australia-2014-139) At 2,228 m (7,310 ft), [Mount Kosciuszko](/wiki/Mount_Kosciuszko "Mount Kosciuszko") is the highest mountain on the mainland. Taller are [Mawson Peak](/wiki/Mawson_Peak "Mawson Peak"), at 2,745 m (9,006 ft), on Heard Island, and, in the Australian Antarctic Territory, [Mount McClintock](/wiki/Mount_McClintock "Mount McClintock") and [Mount Menzies](/wiki/Mount_Menzies "Mount Menzies"), at 3,492 m (11,457 ft) and 3,355 m (11,007 ft) respectively.[[133]](#cite_note-Geoscience_Australia-2014-139)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Uluru%2C_helicopter_view%2C_cropped.jpg/250px-Uluru%2C_helicopter_view%2C_cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:Uluru,_helicopter_view,_cropped.jpg)

[Uluru](/wiki/Uluru "Uluru") in the semi-arid region of Central Australia

The [Murray-Darling](/wiki/Murray%E2%80%93Darling_basin "Murray–Darling basin") is the major river system, draining most of inland New South Wales and Southern Queensland towards [Lake Alexandrina](/wiki/Lake_Alexandrina_(South_Australia) "Lake Alexandrina (South Australia)") and the sea in South Australia. There are also smaller coastal river systems, inland drainage systems such as the [Lake Eyre](/wiki/Lake_Eyre_basin "Lake Eyre basin") system, and salt lake systems in central and western Australia.[[134]](#cite_note-140) Australia's rivers have the lowest discharge into the sea of any continent. The mainland's flat, arid profile also makes its rivers slow-moving, resulting in a build up of salt on the land.[[135]](#cite_note-141) Salinisation adversely affects Australia's soil which is, on average, poor in nutrients compared with world standards.[[136]](#cite_note-142)

Australia's population is concentrated on the coastal fringes. About 95% of the population lives within 100 km of the coast; the world average is 39%.[[137]](#cite_note-Blewett_2012-2-143) Australia's [population density](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_dependencies_by_population_density "List of countries and dependencies by population density") is 3.5 inhabitants per square kilometre,[[18]](#cite_note-Australian_Bureau_of_Statistics-2023-24) which is one of the lowest in the world.[[137]](#cite_note-Blewett_2012-2-143) However, there is a large concentration of the population in cities along the temperate south-eastern coastline, and population density exceeds 38 inhabitants per square kilometre in central Melbourne.[[18]](#cite_note-Australian_Bureau_of_Statistics-2023-24)

### Geology

Main article: [Geology of Australia](/wiki/Geology_of_Australia "Geology of Australia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/Ausgeolbasic.jpg/330px-Ausgeolbasic.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ausgeolbasic.jpg)

Basic geological regions of Australia (by age)

Formerly part of the [Rodinia](/wiki/Rodinia "Rodinia") and [Gondwana](/wiki/Gondwana "Gondwana") [supercontinents](/wiki/Supercontinent "Supercontinent"),[[138]](#cite_note-144) Australia completely separated from Antarctica about 35 million years ago and continued drifting northwards.[[139]](#cite_note-Kennett-145) When the [Last Glacial Period](/wiki/Last_Glacial_Period "Last Glacial Period") ended, rising sea levels separated the Australian mainland from New Guinea about 8,000 years ago and from [Tasmania](/wiki/Tasmania "Tasmania") about 6,000 years ago.[[139]](#cite_note-Kennett-145)

Australia lies well within the [Australian tectonic plate](/wiki/Australian_plate "Australian plate"). The mainland is relatively stable geologically, with no major mountain building, active volcanoes or tectonic faults.[[140]](#cite_note-146) However, the Australian plate is moving north-northeast at a rate of about 6 to 7 cm a year and is currently in collision with the [Eurasian plate](/wiki/Eurasian_plate "Eurasian plate") and [Pacific plate](/wiki/Pacific_plate "Pacific plate").[[141]](#cite_note-Kennett-2-147) The resulting intratectonic stresses lead to relatively high seismic activity for a geologically stable landmass. There were 18 earthquakes with a [moment magnitude](/wiki/Moment_magnitude_scale "Moment magnitude scale") of greater than 6 between 1901 and 2017.[[141]](#cite_note-Kennett-2-147) The [Newcastle earthquake](/wiki/1989_Newcastle_earthquake "1989 Newcastle earthquake") of 1989 was Australia's deadliest, killing 13 people.[[142]](#cite_note-148) There were active volcanoes on the eastern mainland as recently as 4,600 years ago,[[143]](#cite_note-Kennett-3-149) and this is reflected in Aboriginal place names and creation stories.[[144]](#cite_note-150) Currently, [volcanism](/wiki/Volcanism "Volcanism") occurs in the remote [Heard Island and McDonald Islands](/wiki/Heard_Island_and_McDonald_Islands "Heard Island and McDonald Islands").[[145]](#cite_note-151)

The Australian continental crust was created in three cycles from the oldest [Archaean](/wiki/Archean "Archean") [cratons](/wiki/Craton "Craton") in the west to the younger [orogenic](/wiki/Orogenic_belt "Orogenic belt") formations in the east (built about 541 million to 252 million years ago).[[143]](#cite_note-Kennett-3-149)[[146]](#cite_note-152) The oldest Australian surface rocks date to the Archaean period. Some in Western Australia are older than 3.7 billion years and others in South Australia are over 3.1 billion years old. The oldest zircon crystals on Earth, dating back 4.4 billion years, have been found in Western Australia. However, about 80 per cent of Australia is covered by sedimentary rocks and [regolith](/wiki/Regolith "Regolith") that are less than 250 million years old.[[143]](#cite_note-Kennett-3-149)

### Climate

Main article: [Climate of Australia](/wiki/Climate_of_Australia "Climate of Australia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Koppen-Geiger_Map_v2_AUS_1991%E2%80%932020.svg/250px-Koppen-Geiger_Map_v2_AUS_1991%E2%80%932020.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Koppen-Geiger_Map_v2_AUS_1991%E2%80%932020.svg)

[Köppen climate types](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") of Australia[[147]](#cite_note-153)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/Kakadu_2534.jpg/250px-Kakadu_2534.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kakadu_2534.jpg)

A [billabong](/wiki/Billabong "Billabong") in the [Kakadu National Park](/wiki/Kakadu_National_Park "Kakadu National Park"), [Northern Territory](/wiki/Northern_Territory "Northern Territory"). The monsoon climate of northern Australia is hot and humid in summer.

The Australian climate ranges from wet tropical in the northeast and northwest to arid in the centre. The coastal south is temperate and humid with winter freezing and snow in the southeastern highlands and Tasmania. The climate is influenced by Australia's position in the "[horse latitudes](/wiki/Horse_latitudes "Horse latitudes")", which tends to bring arid conditions.[[148]](#cite_note-Henderson-154) Overall, the Australian mainland is the driest inhabited continent, with an average annual rainfall of 470 millimetres (19 in).[[128]](#cite_note-Blewett_2012-3-134) About 70% of the country is arid or semi-arid,[[128]](#cite_note-Blewett_2012-3-134) and about 18% is desert.[[149]](#cite_note-155)

The climate is also influenced by various systems such as the [El Niño–Southern Oscillation](/wiki/El_Ni%C3%B1o%E2%80%93Southern_Oscillation "El Niño–Southern Oscillation"), the [Indian Ocean Dipole](/wiki/Indian_Ocean_Dipole "Indian Ocean Dipole") and the [Southern Annular Mode](/wiki/Antarctic_oscillation "Antarctic oscillation").[[150]](#cite_note-Australian_Government,_Bureau_of_Meteorology-156) Australia has unusual variability in rainfall within years and between years, leading to frequent droughts and flooding. Cyclones and rain depressions are common in tropical Australia. The summer monsoon brings significant rainfall to northern Australia and low pressure cells bring winter rainfall in the south. The hottest regions are in the northwest of the country and the coolest in the southeast. Bushfire conditions are common in southern Australia.[[148]](#cite_note-Henderson-154)

[Climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change") from increased [greenhouse gas emissions](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas_emissions "Greenhouse gas emissions") has led to a 1.5 °C rise in Australian temperatures since 1910 and an increase in extreme heat and heavy rainfall events. There has been a reduction in rainfall from April to October in southern Australia since 1970 and a longer bushfire season since the 1950s. Rainfall has increased in northern Australia since the 1970s. The number of tropical cyclones has fallen since 1982 and alpine snow has decreased since the late 1950s. Sea levels are rising around Australia and the surrounding oceans are becoming more acidic.[[150]](#cite_note-Australian_Government,_Bureau_of_Meteorology-156)

### Biodiversity

See also: [Fauna of Australia](/wiki/Fauna_of_Australia "Fauna of Australia"), [Flora of Australia](/wiki/Flora_of_Australia "Flora of Australia"), and [Fungi of Australia](/wiki/Fungi_of_Australia "Fungi of Australia")

[![A koala holding onto a eucalyptus tree with its head turned so both eyes are visible](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Koala_climbing_tree.jpg/250px-Koala_climbing_tree.jpg)](/wiki/File:Koala_climbing_tree.jpg)

[Koala](/wiki/Koala "Koala") and *[Eucalyptus](/wiki/Eucalyptus "Eucalyptus")*

Australia is one of 17 [megadiverse countries](/wiki/Megadiverse_countries "Megadiverse countries").[[151]](#cite_note-157) Because of its long geographic isolation, much of Australia's [biota](/wiki/Biota_(ecology) "Biota (ecology)") is unique.[[152]](#cite_note-158) About 94% of its [amphibians](/wiki/Amphibians_of_Australia "Amphibians of Australia"), 93% of its [reptiles](/wiki/List_of_reptiles_of_Australia "List of reptiles of Australia") and [flowering plants](/wiki/Flora_of_Australia "Flora of Australia"), 69% of its [mammals](/wiki/Mammals_of_Australia "Mammals of Australia") and 46% of its [birds](/wiki/Birds_of_Australia "Birds of Australia") are [endemic](/wiki/Endemism "Endemism").[[153]](#cite_note-159) Australia has a wide range of [ecosystems](/wiki/Ecosystem "Ecosystem") of which 89 regions and 419 subregions are recognised in the Australian bioregion framework.[[154]](#cite_note-160)[[155]](#cite_note-161)

In January 2025, there were 168,386 named species on the Australian National Species List.[[156]](#cite_note-162) However, it is estimated that 70% of Australian species have not been discovered and classified and that there may be 600,000 Australian native species.[[157]](#cite_note-Murphy-163) In general, knowledge of [vertebrates](/wiki/Vertebrate "Vertebrate") and flowering plants is better than for [invertebrates](/wiki/Invertebrate "Invertebrate") and [fungi](/wiki/Fungus "Fungus"). It is estimated that less that 10% of [Australia's fungi](/wiki/Fungi_of_Australia "Fungi of Australia") and insects have been named.[[157]](#cite_note-Murphy-163)

About 10% of the world's known plant species are found in Australia.[[158]](#cite_note-164) Many of these have adapted to the arid climate, variable rainfall and nutrient-poor soil. [Deserts and xeric shrubland](/wiki/Deserts_and_xeric_shrublands "Deserts and xeric shrublands") cover about 70% of the mainland. [Acacia](/wiki/Acacia "Acacia"), [banksia](/wiki/Banksia "Banksia") and [eucalypts](/wiki/Eucalypt "Eucalypt") have spread over much of Australia. Many plants have hard and long-living leaves, and are rich in carbon, poor in nutrients, and well adapted to bushfires.[[159]](#cite_note-165)

About two-thirds of the world's 330 species of [marsupials](/wiki/Marsupial "Marsupial") are native to Australia.[[160]](#cite_note-166) Australian [placental mammals](/wiki/Placental_mammals "Placental mammals") (overwhelmingly bats, rats and mice) also make up almost 47% of the world's land mammal species.[[161]](#cite_note-167) Australia has about 10% of the world's known reptile species.[[162]](#cite_note-168) There are also about 320,500 invertebrate species, of which insects are the largest class, accounting for more than 75% of all animal species.[[163]](#cite_note-169) Australia has over 15,000 known species of fungi, although it is possible that tens of thousands more exist.[[164]](#cite_note-170)

Australia's wildlife show many adaptations to their environments. As the leaves of most plants are poor in nutrients, Australia has a high proportion of birds, insects and marsupials, such as the [honey possum](/wiki/Honey_possum "Honey possum"), that feed on nectar and pollen. The [koala](/wiki/Koala "Koala") is an exception, specialising in feeding on eucalyptus leaves.[[165]](#cite_note-171)[[166]](#cite_note-Nipperess_2015-172) Nutritionally poor flora and variable rainfall also favour animals with lower energy requirements, including snakes, lizards, and hopping marsupials such as the [kangaroo](/wiki/Kangaroo "Kangaroo") and [wallaby](/wiki/Wallaby "Wallaby"). There is, however, evidence of [convergent evolution](/wiki/Convergent_evolution "Convergent evolution") of Australia's marsupials and the placental mammals of other continents living in similar environments. For example, the extinct [thylacine](/wiki/Thylacine "Thylacine") (Tasmanian tiger) had similarities with the placental wolf, [marsupial moles](/wiki/Marsupial_mole "Marsupial mole") with the [golden moles](/wiki/Golden_mole "Golden mole") of Africa, and [hopping mice](/wiki/Hopping_mouse "Hopping mouse") with the hopping rodents of other arid regions.[[166]](#cite_note-Nipperess_2015-172)

There were major extinctions of Australia's vertebrates, including its [megafauna](/wiki/Australian_megafauna "Australian megafauna"), around 46 thousand years ago, and there is an ongoing scientific debate over the role of human activity and climate change in these extinctions. The contraction of the range of the Tasmanian tiger and [Tasmanian devil](/wiki/Tasmanian_devil "Tasmanian devil") to that island around 4,000 to 5,000 years ago is also consistent with changes on the mainland including an increasing human population, the introduction of the [dingo](/wiki/Dingo "Dingo"), and the greater use of fire and new stone tool technologies.[[167]](#cite_note-173)[[168]](#cite_note-174)

Over the past two centuries, Australia has lost more mammal species than any other continent. Overall, 100 Australian species are listed as extinct or extinct in the wild. In June 2021, over 1,000 animal and plant species were listed by Australian governments as endangered or critically endangered.[[169]](#cite_note-175) The major threats to endangered species are landscape change, ecosystem disruption, introduced species such as the [feral cat](/wiki/Cats_in_Australia "Cats in Australia") and [red fox](/wiki/Red_foxes_in_Australia "Red foxes in Australia"), and climate change.[[170]](#cite_note-176)

The federal *[Environment Protection and Biodiversity Conservation Act 1999](/wiki/Environment_Protection_and_Biodiversity_Conservation_Act_1999 "Environment Protection and Biodiversity Conservation Act 1999")* is the legal framework for the protection of threatened species.[[171]](#cite_note-177) The [National Reserve System](/wiki/National_Reserve_System "National Reserve System") is Australia's network of protected areas. As at 30 June 2022, it covered over 22% of Australia's land mass.[[172]](#cite_note-178) *Australia's Strategy for Nature 2024–2030* is the national biodiversity plan that aims to reverse biodiversity loss in Australia by 2030 and meet the county's obligations under the [United Nations Convention on Biological Diversity](/wiki/Convention_on_Biological_Diversity "Convention on Biological Diversity") and other international agreements.[[173]](#cite_note-179)[[174]](#cite_note-180)

## Government and politics

Main articles: [Australian Government](/wiki/Australian_Government "Australian Government"), [Politics of Australia](/wiki/Politics_of_Australia "Politics of Australia"), and [Monarchy of Australia](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Australia "Monarchy of Australia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/King_Charles_III_%28July_2023%29.jpg/250px-King_Charles_III_%28July_2023%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:King_Charles_III_(July_2023).jpg)

[Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III")  
[King of Australia](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Australia "Monarchy of Australia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Sam_Mostyn_2024.jpg/250px-Sam_Mostyn_2024.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sam_Mostyn_2024.jpg)

[Sam Mostyn](/wiki/Sam_Mostyn "Sam Mostyn")  
[Governor-General](/wiki/Governor-General_of_Australia "Governor-General of Australia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/Anthony_Albanese_portrait_%28re-crop%29.jpg/250px-Anthony_Albanese_portrait_%28re-crop%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Anthony_Albanese_portrait_(re-crop).jpg)

[Anthony Albanese](/wiki/Anthony_Albanese "Anthony Albanese")  
[Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Australia "Prime Minister of Australia")

Australia is a [constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy"), a [parliamentary democracy](/wiki/Parliamentary_democracy "Parliamentary democracy") and a [federation](/wiki/Federation "Federation").[[175]](#cite_note-181) The country has maintained its mostly unchanged [constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Australia "Constitution of Australia") alongside a stable [liberal democratic](/wiki/Liberal_democracy "Liberal democracy") political system since [Federation](/wiki/Federation_of_Australia "Federation of Australia") in 1901. It is one of the world's oldest federations, in which power is divided between the federal and [state](/wiki/States_and_territories_of_Australia "States and territories of Australia") governments. The [Australian system of government](/wiki/Politics_of_Australia "Politics of Australia") combines elements derived from the political systems of the United Kingdom (a [fused executive](/wiki/Fusion_of_powers "Fusion of powers"), constitutional monarchy and [strong party discipline](/wiki/Party_discipline "Party discipline")) and the United States ([federalism](/wiki/Federalism "Federalism"), a [written constitution](/wiki/Written_constitution "Written constitution") and [strong bicameralism](/wiki/Bicameralism "Bicameralism") with a Senate in which states have equal representation), resulting in a distinct hybrid.[[176]](#cite_note-Thompson-1980-182)[[177]](#cite_note-183)

[Federal government power is partially separated](/wiki/Separation_of_powers_in_Australia "Separation of powers in Australia") between three groups:[[178]](#cite_note-184)

* Legislature: the bicameral [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Australia "Parliament of Australia"), comprising the [monarch](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Australia "Monarchy of Australia"), the [Senate](/wiki/Australian_Senate "Australian Senate"), and the [House of Representatives](/wiki/Australian_House_of_Representatives "Australian House of Representatives")
* Executive: the [Australian Government](/wiki/Australian_Government "Australian Government"), led by the prime minister (the leader of the party or coalition with a majority in the House of Representatives), their chosen [Cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Australia "Cabinet of Australia") and other ministers; formally appointed by the governor-general[[179]](#cite_note-The_World_Factbook-185)
* Judiciary: the [High Court](/wiki/High_Court_of_Australia "High Court of Australia") and other [federal courts](/wiki/Australian_court_hierarchy "Australian court hierarchy")

Following elections on 3 May 2025, the prime minister is [Anthony Albanese](/wiki/Anthony_Albanese "Anthony Albanese") of the [Australian Labor Party](/wiki/Australian_Labor_Party "Australian Labor Party").[[180]](#cite_note-186) [Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III") reigns as [King of Australia](/wiki/King_of_Australia "King of Australia") and is represented in Australia by the [governor-general](/wiki/Governor-General_of_Australia "Governor-General of Australia") at the federal level and by the [governors](/wiki/Governors_of_the_Australian_states "Governors of the Australian states") at the state level, who by [section 63](/wiki/Chapter_II_of_the_Constitution_of_Australia#Section_63:_Provisions_referring_to_Governor-General "Chapter II of the Constitution of Australia") of the Constitution and convention act on the advice of their ministers.[[181]](#cite_note-187)[[182]](#cite_note-Governor-General_of_Australia-188) Thus, in practice the governor-general acts as a legal figurehead for the actions of the [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Australia "Prime Minister of Australia") and the Cabinet. The governor-general may in some situations exercise [reserve powers](/wiki/Reserve_power "Reserve power"): powers exercisable in the absence of, or contrary to, ministerial advice. When these powers may be exercised is governed by convention and their precise scope is unclear. The most notable exercise of these powers was the dismissal of the [Whitlam government](/wiki/Whitlam_government "Whitlam government") in the [constitutional crisis of 1975](/wiki/1975_Australian_constitutional_crisis "1975 Australian constitutional crisis").[[183]](#cite_note-189)

[![A large white and cream coloured building with grass on its roof. The building is topped with a large flagpole.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Canberra_%28AU%29%2C_Parliament_House_--_2019_--_1746.jpg/250px-Canberra_%28AU%29%2C_Parliament_House_--_2019_--_1746.jpg)](/wiki/File:Canberra_(AU),_Parliament_House_--_2019_--_1746.jpg)

[Parliament House](/wiki/Parliament_House,_Canberra "Parliament House, Canberra"), [Canberra](/wiki/Canberra "Canberra"), [Australian Capital Territory](/wiki/Australian_Capital_Territory "Australian Capital Territory")

In the Senate (the upper house), there are 76 senators: twelve each from the states and two each from the mainland territories (the Australian Capital Territory and the Northern Territory).[[184]](#cite_note-Australian_Broadcasting_Corporation-190) The House of Representatives (the lower house) has 150 members elected from single-member [electoral divisions](/wiki/Divisions_of_the_Australian_House_of_Representatives "Divisions of the Australian House of Representatives"), commonly known as "electorates" or "seats", allocated to states on the basis of population, with each of the current states guaranteed a minimum of five seats.[[185]](#cite_note-191) The lower house has a maximum term of three years, but this is not fixed and governments usually dissolve the house early for an election at some point in the 6 months before the maximum.[[186]](#cite_note-192) Elections for both chambers are generally held simultaneously with senators having overlapping six-year terms except for those from the territories, whose terms are not fixed but are tied to the electoral cycle for the lower house. Thus, only 40 of the 76 places in the Senate are put to each election unless the cycle is interrupted by a [double dissolution](/wiki/Double_dissolution "Double dissolution").[[184]](#cite_note-Australian_Broadcasting_Corporation-190)

Australia's [electoral system](/wiki/Electoral_system_of_Australia "Electoral system of Australia") uses [preferential voting](/wiki/Instant-runoff_voting "Instant-runoff voting") for the House of Representatives and all state and territory lower house elections (with the exception of Tasmania and the ACT which use the [Hare-Clark system](/wiki/Hare-Clark_system "Hare-Clark system")). The Senate and most state upper houses use the [proportional system](/wiki/Single_transferable_vote "Single transferable vote") which combines preferential voting with [proportional representation](/wiki/Proportional_representation "Proportional representation") for each state. [Voting and enrolment is compulsory](/wiki/Compulsory_voting "Compulsory voting") for all enrolled citizens 18 years and older in every jurisdiction.[[187]](#cite_note-193)[[188]](#cite_note-194)[[189]](#cite_note-195) The party with majority support in the House of Representatives forms the government and its leader becomes prime minister. The governor-general appoints the prime minister and may dismiss one who has lost the confidence of parliament or acts illegally.[[190]](#cite_note-196) As Australia is a [Westminster](/wiki/Westminster_system "Westminster system") parliamentary democracy with a powerful and elected upper house, its system has sometimes been called a "Washminster mutation",[[176]](#cite_note-Thompson-1980-182) or semi-parliamentary.[[191]](#cite_note-197)

There are two major political groups that have usually formed government federally: the [Australian Labor Party](/wiki/Australian_Labor_Party "Australian Labor Party") and the [Coalition](/wiki/Coalition_(Australia) "Coalition (Australia)") which is a grouping of the [Liberal Party](/wiki/Liberal_Party_of_Australia "Liberal Party of Australia") and its minor partner, the [National Party](/wiki/National_Party_of_Australia "National Party of Australia").[[192]](#cite_note-198)[[193]](#cite_note-199) At the state level of government, the relationship between the Nationals and the Liberal Party differs, with the parties [merged in Queensland](/wiki/Liberal_National_Party_of_Queensland "Liberal National Party of Queensland") and the [Northern Territory](/wiki/Country_Liberal_Party "Country Liberal Party") (federal parliamentarians, however, sit in either the Liberal or National party room); in coalition in New South Wales, Victoria and Western Australia; and in competition with the Liberals in South Australia and Tasmania.[[194]](#cite_note-200) Within Australian political culture, the Labor Party is considered [centre-left](/wiki/Centre-left "Centre-left") and the Coalition is considered [centre-right](/wiki/Centre-right "Centre-right").[[195]](#cite_note-201) Independent members and several minor parties have achieved representation in Australian parliaments, mostly in upper houses. The [Australian Greens](/wiki/Australian_Greens "Australian Greens") have been the third largest party by both vote and membership since 2004.[[196]](#cite_note-202)[[197]](#cite_note-203)

### States and territories

Main article: [States and territories of Australia](/wiki/States_and_territories_of_Australia "States and territories of Australia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Australia_states_and_territories_labelled.svg/500px-Australia_states_and_territories_labelled.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Australia_states_and_territories_labelled.svg)

Australia's states and territories

Australia has six states – [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales") (NSW), [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria_(state) "Victoria (state)") (Vic), [Queensland](/wiki/Queensland "Queensland") (Qld), [Western Australia](/wiki/Western_Australia "Western Australia") (WA), [South Australia](/wiki/South_Australia "South Australia") (SA) and [Tasmania](/wiki/Tasmania "Tasmania") (Tas) – and two mainland self-governing territories – the [Australian Capital Territory](/wiki/Australian_Capital_Territory "Australian Capital Territory") (ACT) and the [Northern Territory](/wiki/Northern_Territory "Northern Territory") (NT).[[198]](#cite_note-204)

The states have the general power to make laws except in the few areas where the constitution grants the Commonwealth (the federal level of government) exclusive powers.[[199]](#cite_note-205)[[200]](#cite_note-206) The Commonwealth can only make laws on topics listed in the constitution but its laws prevail over those of the states to the extent of any inconsistency.[[201]](#cite_note-207)[[202]](#cite_note-208) Since Federation, the Commonwealth's power relative to the states [has significantly increased](/wiki/Australian_constitutional_law#Growth_of_federal_power "Australian constitutional law") due to the increasingly wide interpretation given to listed Commonwealth powers – and because of the states' [heavy financial reliance](/wiki/Fiscal_imbalance_in_Australia "Fiscal imbalance in Australia") on Commonwealth grants.[[203]](#cite_note-209)[[204]](#cite_note-210)

Each state and major mainland territory has [its own parliament](/wiki/Parliaments_of_the_Australian_states_and_territories "Parliaments of the Australian states and territories") – [unicameral](/wiki/Unicameral "Unicameral") in the Northern Territory, the ACT and Queensland, and bicameral in the other states. The lower houses are known as the [Legislative Assembly](/wiki/Legislative_Assembly "Legislative Assembly") (the [House of Assembly](/wiki/House_of_Assembly "House of Assembly") in South Australia and Tasmania); the upper houses are known as the [Legislative Council](/wiki/Legislative_council "Legislative council"). The [head of the government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government") in each state is the [premier](/wiki/Premiers_of_the_Australian_states "Premiers of the Australian states") and in each territory the [chief minister](/wiki/Chief_Minister "Chief Minister"). The King is represented in each state by a [governor](/wiki/Governors_of_the_Australian_states "Governors of the Australian states"). At the Commonwealth level, the King's representative is the governor-general.[[182]](#cite_note-Governor-General_of_Australia-188)

The Commonwealth government directly administers the internal [Jervis Bay Territory](/wiki/Jervis_Bay_Territory "Jervis Bay Territory") and the external territories: the [Ashmore and Cartier Islands](/wiki/Ashmore_and_Cartier_Islands "Ashmore and Cartier Islands"), the [Coral Sea Islands](/wiki/Coral_Sea_Islands "Coral Sea Islands"), the [Heard Island and McDonald Islands](/wiki/Heard_Island_and_McDonald_Islands "Heard Island and McDonald Islands"), the [Indian Ocean territories](/wiki/Australian_Indian_Ocean_Territories "Australian Indian Ocean Territories") (Christmas Island and the Cocos (Keeling) Islands), [Norfolk Island](/wiki/Norfolk_Island "Norfolk Island"),[[207]](#cite_note-213) and the [Australian Antarctic Territory](/wiki/Australian_Antarctic_Territory "Australian Antarctic Territory").[[208]](#cite_note-214)[[209]](#cite_note-215)[[179]](#cite_note-The_World_Factbook-185) The remote [Macquarie Island](/wiki/Macquarie_Island "Macquarie Island") and [Lord Howe Island](/wiki/Lord_Howe_Island "Lord Howe Island") are part of Tasmania and New South Wales respectively.[[210]](#cite_note-216)[[211]](#cite_note-217)

### Foreign relations

Main article: [Foreign relations of Australia](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_Australia "Foreign relations of Australia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Diplomatic_missions_of_Australia.png/330px-Diplomatic_missions_of_Australia.png)](/wiki/File:Diplomatic_missions_of_Australia.png)

[Diplomatic missions of Australia](/wiki/List_of_diplomatic_missions_of_Australia "List of diplomatic missions of Australia")

Australia is a [middle power](/wiki/Middle_power "Middle power"),[[212]](#cite_note-218) whose foreign relations has three core bi-partisan pillars: commitment to the US alliance, engagement with the [Indo-Pacific](/wiki/Indo-Pacific "Indo-Pacific") and support for international institutions, rules and co-operation.[[213]](#cite_note-219)[[214]](#cite_note-Australian_Government-2017-220)[[215]](#cite_note-221) Through the [ANZUS](/wiki/ANZUS "ANZUS") pact and its status as a [major non-NATO ally](/wiki/Major_non-NATO_ally "Major non-NATO ally"), Australia maintains a [close relationship with the US](/wiki/Australia%E2%80%93United_States_relations "Australia–United States relations"), which encompasses strong defence, security and trade ties.[[216]](#cite_note-222)[[217]](#cite_note-223) In the Indo-Pacific, the country seeks to increase its trade ties through the open flow of trade and capital, while managing the rise of Chinese power by supporting the existing rules-based order.[[214]](#cite_note-Australian_Government-2017-220) Regionally, the country is a member of the [Pacific Islands Forum](/wiki/Pacific_Islands_Forum "Pacific Islands Forum"), the [Pacific Community](/wiki/Pacific_Community "Pacific Community"), the [ASEAN+6 mechanism](/wiki/Association_of_Southeast_Asian_Nations#ASEAN_Plus_Three_and_ASEAN_Plus_Six "Association of Southeast Asian Nations") and the [East Asia Summit](/wiki/East_Asia_Summit "East Asia Summit"). Internationally, the country is a member of the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") (of which it was a founding member), the [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations"), the [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD") and the [G20](/wiki/G20 "G20"). This reflects the country's generally strong commitment to [multilateralism](/wiki/Multilateralism "Multilateralism").[[218]](#cite_note-224)[[219]](#cite_note-225)

Australia is a member of several defence, intelligence and security groupings including the [Five Eyes](/wiki/Five_Eyes "Five Eyes") intelligence alliance with the United States, United Kingdom, Canada and New Zealand; the ANZUS alliance with the United States and New Zealand; the [AUKUS](/wiki/AUKUS "AUKUS") security treaty with the United States and United Kingdom; the [Quadrilateral Security Dialogue](/wiki/Quadrilateral_Security_Dialogue "Quadrilateral Security Dialogue") with the United States, India and Japan; the [Five Power Defence Arrangements](/wiki/Five_Power_Defence_Arrangements "Five Power Defence Arrangements") with New Zealand, the United Kingdom, Malaysia and Singapore; and the [Reciprocal Access](/wiki/Reciprocal_Access_Agreement "Reciprocal Access Agreement") defence and security agreement with Japan.[[220]](#cite_note-226)[[221]](#cite_note-227)[[222]](#cite_note-228)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/P20220524AS-1533_%2852245766080%29.jpg/250px-P20220524AS-1533_%2852245766080%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:P20220524AS-1533_(52245766080).jpg)

Australian Prime Minister [Anthony Albanese](/wiki/Anthony_Albanese "Anthony Albanese") with American President [Joe Biden](/wiki/Joe_Biden "Joe Biden") in 2022

Australia has pursued the cause of international [trade liberalisation](/wiki/Trade_liberalisation "Trade liberalisation").[[223]](#cite_note-229) It led the formation of the [Cairns Group](/wiki/Cairns_Group "Cairns Group") and [Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation](/wiki/Asia-Pacific_Economic_Cooperation "Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation"),[[224]](#cite_note-230)[[225]](#cite_note-231) and is a member of the [Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development](/wiki/OECD "OECD") (OECD) and the [World Trade Organization](/wiki/World_Trade_Organization "World Trade Organization") (WTO).[[226]](#cite_note-232)[[227]](#cite_note-233) Beginning in the 2000s, Australia entered into the [Comprehensive and Progressive Agreement for Trans-Pacific Partnership](/wiki/Comprehensive_and_Progressive_Agreement_for_Trans-Pacific_Partnership "Comprehensive and Progressive Agreement for Trans-Pacific Partnership") and the [Regional Comprehensive Economic Partnership](/wiki/Regional_Comprehensive_Economic_Partnership "Regional Comprehensive Economic Partnership") multilateral [free trade agreements](/wiki/Free_trade_agreement "Free trade agreement") as well as bilateral free trade agreements with the [United States](/wiki/Australia%E2%80%93United_States_Free_Trade_Agreement "Australia–United States Free Trade Agreement"), [China](/wiki/China%E2%80%93Australia_Free_Trade_Agreement "China–Australia Free Trade Agreement"), [Japan](/wiki/Japan%E2%80%93Australia_Economic_Partnership_Agreement "Japan–Australia Economic Partnership Agreement"), [South Korea](/wiki/Australia%E2%80%93Korea_Free_Trade_Agreement "Australia–Korea Free Trade Agreement"), [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia%E2%80%93Australia_Comprehensive_Economic_Partnership_Agreement "Indonesia–Australia Comprehensive Economic Partnership Agreement"), the [United Kingdom](/wiki/Australia%E2%80%93United_Kingdom_Free_Trade_Agreement "Australia–United Kingdom Free Trade Agreement") and [New Zealand](/wiki/Closer_Economic_Relations "Closer Economic Relations"), with the most recent deal signed in 2023 with the UK.[[228]](#cite_note-234)

Australia maintains a deeply integrated relationship with neighbouring New Zealand, with free mobility of citizens between the two countries under the [Trans-Tasman Travel Arrangement](/wiki/Trans-Tasman_Travel_Arrangement "Trans-Tasman Travel Arrangement") and free trade under the [Closer Economic Relations](/wiki/Closer_Economic_Relations "Closer Economic Relations") agreement.[[229]](#cite_note-235) The most favourably viewed countries by the Australian people in 2021 include New Zealand, the United Kingdom, Japan, Germany, Taiwan, Thailand, the United States and South Korea.[[230]](#cite_note-236) It also maintains an [international aid program](/wiki/International_aid "International aid") under which some 75 countries receive assistance.[[231]](#cite_note-237) Australia ranked fourth in the [Center for Global Development](/wiki/Center_for_Global_Development "Center for Global Development")'s 2021 [Commitment to Development Index](/wiki/Commitment_to_Development_Index "Commitment to Development Index").[[232]](#cite_note-238)

The power over foreign policy is highly concentrated in the prime minister and the [national security committee](/wiki/National_Security_Committee_(Australia) "National Security Committee (Australia)"), with major decision such as joining the [2003 invasion of Iraq](/wiki/2003_invasion_of_Iraq "2003 invasion of Iraq") made without prior Cabinet approval.[[233]](#cite_note-239)[[234]](#cite_note-240) Similarly, the Parliament does not play a formal role in foreign policy and the power to declare war lies solely with the executive government.[[235]](#cite_note-241) The [Department of Foreign Affairs and Trade](/wiki/Department_of_Foreign_Affairs_and_Trade_(Australia) "Department of Foreign Affairs and Trade (Australia)") supports the executive in its policy decisions.[[236]](#cite_note-242)

### Military

Main article: [Australian Defence Force](/wiki/Australian_Defence_Force "Australian Defence Force")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/HMAS_Arunta_and_Canberra_sailing_in_formation_with_other_warships.jpg/250px-HMAS_Arunta_and_Canberra_sailing_in_formation_with_other_warships.jpg)](/wiki/File:HMAS_Arunta_and_Canberra_sailing_in_formation_with_other_warships.jpg)

[HMAS *Canberra*](/wiki/HMAS_Canberra_(L02) "HMAS Canberra (L02)") (L02), a [*Canberra*-class](/wiki/Canberra-class_landing_helicopter_dock "Canberra-class landing helicopter dock") [landing helicopter dock](/wiki/Landing_helicopter_dock "Landing helicopter dock"), and [HMAS *Arunta*](/wiki/HMAS_Arunta_(FFH_151) "HMAS Arunta (FFH 151)"), an [*Anzac*-class](/wiki/Anzac-class_frigate "Anzac-class frigate") [frigate](/wiki/Frigate "Frigate"), sailing in formation
