The two main institutions involved in the management of Australia's armed forces are the [Australian Defence Force](/wiki/Australian_Defence_Force "Australian Defence Force") (ADF) and the [Department of Defence](/wiki/Department_of_Defence_(Australia) "Department of Defence (Australia)"), together known as "[Defence](/wiki/Australian_Defence_Organisation "Australian Defence Organisation")".[[237]](#cite_note-243) The Australian Defence Force is the military wing, headed by the [chief of the defence force](/wiki/Chief_of_the_Defence_Force_(Australia) "Chief of the Defence Force (Australia)"), and contains three branches: the [Royal Australian Navy](/wiki/Royal_Australian_Navy "Royal Australian Navy"), the [Australian Army](/wiki/Australian_Army "Australian Army") and the [Royal Australian Air Force](/wiki/Royal_Australian_Air_Force "Royal Australian Air Force"). In 2021, it had 84,865 currently serving personnel (including 60,286 regulars and 24,581 reservists).[[238]](#cite_note-244) The Department of Defence is the civilian wing and is headed by the secretary of defence. These two leaders collective manage Defence as a [diarchy](/wiki/Diarchy "Diarchy"), with shared and joint responsibilities.[[239]](#cite_note-245) The titular role of [commander-in-chief](/wiki/Commander-in-Chief "Commander-in-Chief") is held by the [governor-general](/wiki/Governor-General_of_Australia "Governor-General of Australia"); however, actual command is vested in the chief of the Defence Force.[[240]](#cite_note-246) The executive branch of the Commonwealth government has overall control of the military through the [minister of defence](/wiki/Minister_for_Defence_(Australia) "Minister for Defence (Australia)"), who is subject to the decisions of Cabinet and its [National Security Committee](/wiki/National_Security_Committee_(Australia) "National Security Committee (Australia)").[[241]](#cite_note-247) Major [Australian intelligence agencies](/wiki/Australian_intelligence_agencies "Australian intelligence agencies") include the [Australian Secret Intelligence Service](/wiki/Australian_Secret_Intelligence_Service "Australian Secret Intelligence Service") (foreign intelligence), the [Australian Signals Directorate](/wiki/Australian_Signals_Directorate "Australian Signals Directorate") (signals intelligence) and the [Australian Security Intelligence Organisation](/wiki/Australian_Security_Intelligence_Organisation "Australian Security Intelligence Organisation") (domestic security).

In 2022, defence spending was 1.9% of [GDP](/wiki/GDP "GDP"), representing the world's [13th-largest defence budget](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_military_expenditures "List of countries by military expenditures").[[242]](#cite_note-248) In 2024, the ADF had active operations in the Middle East and the Indo-Pacific (including security and aid provisions); was contributing to UN forces in relation to [South Sudan](/wiki/United_Nations_Mission_in_South_Sudan "United Nations Mission in South Sudan"), [Syria–Israel peacekeeping](/wiki/United_Nations_Disengagement_Observer_Force "United Nations Disengagement Observer Force"), and [North Korea](/wiki/Operation_Argos "Operation Argos"); and domestically was assisting in [natural disaster](/wiki/Natural_disaster "Natural disaster") relief and [assisting in preventing asylum-seekers from entering the country](/wiki/Operation_Resolute "Operation Resolute").[[243]](#cite_note-249)

### Human rights

See also: [Human rights in Australia](/wiki/Human_rights_in_Australia "Human rights in Australia")

Australia has generally strong protections for [civil and political rights](/wiki/Civil_and_political_rights "Civil and political rights"), and the country has signed up to a wide range of international rights treaties.[[244]](#cite_note-250) Important documents protecting human rights include the [Constitution](/wiki/Australian_Constitution "Australian Constitution"), the *[Racial Discrimination Act 1975](/wiki/Racial_Discrimination_Act_1975 "Racial Discrimination Act 1975")*, the *[Sex Discrimination Act 1984](/wiki/Sex_Discrimination_Act_1984 "Sex Discrimination Act 1984")*, the *[Disability Discrimination Act 1992](/wiki/Disability_Discrimination_Act_1992 "Disability Discrimination Act 1992")*, and the *[Age Discrimination Act 2004](/wiki/Age_Discrimination_Act_2004 "Age Discrimination Act 2004")*.[[245]](#cite_note-251) [Same-sex marriage](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage_in_Australia "Same-sex marriage in Australia") has been legal in the nation since 2017.[[246]](#cite_note-252)[[247]](#cite_note-253) Unlike other comparable Western democracies, Australia does not have a single federal [charter of rights](/wiki/Charter_of_rights "Charter of rights") in the Constitution or under legislation; however, the ACT, Victoria, and Queensland have state-based ones.[[248]](#cite_note-254)

International organisations such as [Human Rights Watch](/wiki/Human_Rights_Watch "Human Rights Watch") and [Amnesty International](/wiki/Amnesty_International "Amnesty International") have expressed concerns in areas including [asylum-seeker policy](/wiki/Immigration_detention_in_Australia "Immigration detention in Australia"), [Indigenous deaths in custody](/wiki/Indigenous_deaths_in_custody "Indigenous deaths in custody"), the lack of entrenched [rights protection](/wiki/Charter_of_rights "Charter of rights"), and [laws restricting protesting](/wiki/Laws_governing_public_demonstrations_in_Australia "Laws governing public demonstrations in Australia").[[249]](#cite_note-255)[[250]](#cite_note-256)

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of Australia](/wiki/Economy_of_Australia "Economy of Australia")

Further information: [Economic history of Australia](/wiki/Economic_history_of_Australia "Economic history of Australia") and [Tourism in Australia](/wiki/Tourism_in_Australia "Tourism in Australia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Sydney_central_business_district_skyline%2C_August_2021.jpg/250px-Sydney_central_business_district_skyline%2C_August_2021.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sydney_central_business_district_skyline,_August_2021.jpg)

The [central business district of Sydney](/wiki/Sydney_central_business_district "Sydney central business district") is the [financial centre](/wiki/Financial_centre "Financial centre") of Australia.

Australia's [mixed-market economy](/wiki/Mixed_economy "Mixed economy") is highly [developed](/wiki/Developed_economy "Developed economy") and rich in [natural resources](/wiki/Mining_in_Australia "Mining in Australia").[[251]](#cite_note-257) It is the world's [fourteenth-largest by nominal terms](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(nominal) "List of countries by GDP (nominal)"), and the [18th-largest](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(PPP) "List of countries by GDP (PPP)") by [PPP](/wiki/Purchasing_power_parity "Purchasing power parity"). As of 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Australia&action=edit), Australia has the [second-highest amount of wealth per adult](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_wealth_per_adult "List of countries by wealth per adult"), after [Luxembourg](/wiki/Luxembourg "Luxembourg"),[[252]](#cite_note-258) and the [thirteenth-highest financial assets per capita](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_financial_assets_per_capita "List of countries by financial assets per capita"),[[253]](#cite_note-259) as well as one of the [highest per capita incomes globally](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GNI_(PPP)_per_capita "List of countries by GNI (PPP) per capita").[[254]](#cite_note-260) It has a labour force of some 13.5 million, with an unemployment rate of 3.5% as of June 2022.[[255]](#cite_note-261) According to the [Australian Council of Social Service](/wiki/Australian_Council_of_Social_Service "Australian Council of Social Service"), the [poverty rate of Australia](/wiki/Poverty_in_Australia "Poverty in Australia") exceeds 13.6% of the population, encompassing over 3.2 million.[[256]](#cite_note-262) It also estimated that there were 774,000 (17.7%) children under the age of 15 living in relative poverty.[[257]](#cite_note-263) The [Australian dollar](/wiki/Australian_dollar "Australian dollar") is the national currency, which is also used by three island states in the Pacific: [Kiribati](/wiki/Kiribati "Kiribati"), [Nauru](/wiki/Nauru "Nauru"), and [Tuvalu](/wiki/Tuvalu "Tuvalu").[[258]](#cite_note-264)

[Australian government debt](/wiki/Australian_government_debt "Australian government debt"), about $963 billion in June 2022, exceeds 45.1% of the country's total GDP, and is the world's [eighth-highest](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_government_debt "List of countries by government debt").[[259]](#cite_note-265) Australia had the [second-highest level](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_household_debt "List of countries by household debt") of [household debt](/wiki/Household_debt "Household debt") in the world in 2020, after Switzerland.[[260]](#cite_note-266) [Its house prices](/wiki/Australian_property_market "Australian property market") are among the highest in the world, especially in the large urban areas.[[261]](#cite_note-267) The large service sector accounts for about 71.2% of total GDP, followed by the industrial sector (25.3%), while [its agriculture sector](/wiki/Agriculture_in_Australia "Agriculture in Australia") makes up 3.6% of total GDP.[[262]](#cite_note-268) Australia is the world's [21st-largest exporter](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_exports "List of countries by exports")[[263]](#cite_note-269) and [24th-largest importer](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_imports "List of countries by imports").[[264]](#cite_note-270) China is Australia's [largest trading partner](/wiki/List_of_the_largest_trading_partners_of_Australia "List of the largest trading partners of Australia"), accounting for roughly 40% of the country's exports and 17.6% of its imports.[[265]](#cite_note-271) Other major export markets include Japan, the United States, and South Korea.[[266]](#cite_note-272)

Australia has high levels of competitiveness and economic freedom, and was ranked tenth in the [Human Development Index](/wiki/Human_Development_Index "Human Development Index") in 2022.[[267]](#cite_note-273) As of 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Australia&action=edit), it is ranked twelfth in the [Index of Economic Freedom](/wiki/Index_of_Economic_Freedom "Index of Economic Freedom").[[268]](#cite_note-274) It attracted 9.5 million international tourists in 2019,[[269]](#cite_note-275) and was [ranked thirteenth](/wiki/World_Tourism_rankings "World Tourism rankings") among the countries of [Asia-Pacific](/wiki/Asia-Pacific "Asia-Pacific") in 2019 for inbound tourism.[[270]](#cite_note-UNWTO_World_Tourism_Barometer-2020-276) The 2021 *[Travel and Tourism Competitiveness Report](/wiki/Travel_and_Tourism_Competitiveness_Report "Travel and Tourism Competitiveness Report")* ranked Australia seventh-highest in the world out of 117 countries.[[271]](#cite_note-277) Its international tourism receipts in 2019 amounted to $45.7 billion.[[270]](#cite_note-UNWTO_World_Tourism_Barometer-2020-276)

### Energy

Main articles: [Energy policy of Australia](/wiki/Energy_policy_of_Australia "Energy policy of Australia") and [Renewable energy in Australia](/wiki/Renewable_energy_in_Australia "Renewable energy in Australia")

In 2021–22, Australia's generation of electricity was sourced from [black coal](/wiki/Bituminous_coal "Bituminous coal") (37.2%), [brown coal](/wiki/Brown_coal "Brown coal") (12%), [natural gas](/wiki/Natural_gas "Natural gas") (18.8%), [hydro](/wiki/Hydroelectricity "Hydroelectricity") (6.5%), [wind](/wiki/Wind_power "Wind power") (11.1%), [solar](/wiki/Solar_power "Solar power") (13.3%), [bio-energy](/wiki/Bioenergy "Bioenergy") (1.2%) and others (1.7%).[[272]](#cite_note-278)[[273]](#cite_note-279) Total consumption of energy in this period was sourced from coal (28.4%), oil (37.3%), gas (27.4%) and renewables (7%).[[274]](#cite_note-280) From 2012 to 2022, the energy sourced from renewables has increased 5.7%, while energy sourced from coal has decreased 2.6%. The use of gas also increased by 1.5% and the use of oil stayed relatively stable with a reduction of only 0.2%.[[275]](#cite_note-281)

In 2020, Australia produced 27.7% of its electricity from renewable sources,[[276]](#cite_note-282) exceeding the [target](/wiki/Renewable_energy_target "Renewable energy target") set by the Commonwealth government in 2009 of 20% renewable energy by 2020.[[277]](#cite_note-283) A new target of 82% per cent renewable energy by 2030 was set in 2022[[278]](#cite_note-284) and a target for [net zero emissions](/wiki/Net_zero_emissions "Net zero emissions") by 2050 was set in 2021.[[279]](#cite_note-285)

### Science and technology

In 2019, Australia spent $35.6 billion on [research and development](/wiki/Research_and_development "Research and development"), allocating about 1.79% of GDP.[[280]](#cite_note-286) A 2022 study by the industry lobby group, The Tech Council of Australia, stated that the Australian technology sector combined contributes $167 billion a year to the economy and employs 861,000 people.[[281]](#cite_note-287) In 2022, [startup ecosystems](/wiki/Startup_ecosystem "Startup ecosystem") in Sydney and Melbourne were valued at $34 billion.[[282]](#cite_note-288) Australia ranked 22nd in the [Global Innovation Index](/wiki/Global_Innovation_Index "Global Innovation Index") 2025.[[283]](#cite_note-289)[[284]](#cite_note-290)

With only 0.3% of the world's population, Australia contributed over 4% of the world's published research in 2020,[[285]](#cite_note-Berthold-2021-291) making it one of the top 10 research contributors in the world.[[286]](#cite_note-292) [CSIRO](/wiki/CSIRO "CSIRO"), Australia's national science agency, contributes 10% of all research in the country, while the rest is carried out by universities.[[285]](#cite_note-Berthold-2021-291) Australian achievements include the invention of [atomic absorption spectroscopy](/wiki/Atomic_absorption_spectroscopy "Atomic absorption spectroscopy"),[[287]](#cite_note-293) the essential components of [Wi-Fi](/wiki/Wi-Fi "Wi-Fi") technology,[[288]](#cite_note-294) and the development of the first commercially successful [polymer banknote](/wiki/Polymer_banknote "Polymer banknote").[[289]](#cite_note-295) As of 2026[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Australia&action=edit), 14 Australian scientists have been awarded the [Nobel Prize](/wiki/Nobel_Prize "Nobel Prize") in physics, chemistry or medicine,[[290]](#cite_note-296) and two have been awarded the [Fields Medal](/wiki/Fields_Medal "Fields Medal").[[291]](#cite_note-297)

Facilities supporting space exploration include the [Square Kilometre Array](/wiki/Square_Kilometre_Array "Square Kilometre Array") and [Australia Telescope Compact Array](/wiki/Australia_Telescope_Compact_Array "Australia Telescope Compact Array") radio telescopes, telescopes such as the [Siding Spring Observatory](/wiki/Siding_Spring_Observatory "Siding Spring Observatory"), and ground stations such as the [Canberra Deep Space Communication Complex](/wiki/Canberra_Deep_Space_Communication_Complex "Canberra Deep Space Communication Complex").[[292]](#cite_note-298)

## Demographics

Main article: [Demographics of Australia](/wiki/Demographics_of_Australia "Demographics of Australia")

The population of Australia is estimated to be 28,370,600 as of 27 March 2026.[[10]](#cite_note-Australian_Bureau_of_Statistics_website-2024-14) It is the 54th[[293]](#cite_note-299) most populous country in the world and the most populous [Oceanian](/wiki/Oceania "Oceania") country.

Australia has a [population density](/wiki/Population_density "Population density") of 3.4 persons per square kilometre of total land area, which makes it one of the [most sparsely populated countries in the world](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_population_density "List of countries by population density"). The population is heavily concentrated on the east coast, and in particular in the south-eastern region between [South East Queensland](/wiki/South_East_Queensland "South East Queensland") to the north-east and [Adelaide](/wiki/Adelaide "Adelaide") to the south-west.[[18]](#cite_note-Australian_Bureau_of_Statistics-2023-24)

Australia is also highly urbanised, with 67% of the population living in the Greater Capital City Statistical Areas (metropolitan areas of the state and mainland territorial capital cities) in 2018.[[294]](#cite_note-300) Metropolitan areas with more than one million inhabitants are [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney"), [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne "Melbourne"), [Brisbane](/wiki/Brisbane "Brisbane"), [Perth](/wiki/Perth "Perth") and [Adelaide](/wiki/Adelaide "Adelaide").[[18]](#cite_note-Australian_Bureau_of_Statistics-2023-24)

In common with many other developed countries, Australia is experiencing a demographic shift towards an older population, with more retirees and fewer people of working age. In 2021, the [average age](/wiki/Median_age "Median age") of the population was 39 years.[[295]](#cite_note-301)

### Ancestry and immigration

Main article: [Immigration to Australia](/wiki/Immigration_to_Australia "Immigration to Australia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Australian_Residents_by_Country_of_Birth_2021_Census.svg/330px-Australian_Residents_by_Country_of_Birth_2021_Census.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Australian_Residents_by_Country_of_Birth_2021_Census.svg)

Australian residents by country of birth (2021 census)

Between 1788 and the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"), the vast majority of [settlers](/wiki/Settlers "Settlers") and [immigrants](/wiki/Immigrants "Immigrants") came from the [British Isles](/wiki/Anglo-Celtic_Australians "Anglo-Celtic Australians") (principally [England](/wiki/English_Australians "English Australians"), [Ireland](/wiki/Irish_Australians "Irish Australians") and [Scotland](/wiki/Scottish_Australians "Scottish Australians")), although Australia had significant immigration from [China](/wiki/Chinese_Australians "Chinese Australians") and [Germany](/wiki/German_Australians "German Australians") during the 19th century. Following Federation in 1901, the [white Australia policy](/wiki/White_Australia_policy "White Australia policy") was strengthened, restricting further migration from these areas. However, this policy was relaxed following WW2, and in the decades following, Australia received [a large wave of immigration](/wiki/Post-war_immigration_to_Australia "Post-war immigration to Australia") from across [Europe](/wiki/European_Australians "European Australians"), with many more immigrants arriving from [Southern](/wiki/Southern_Europe "Southern Europe") and [Eastern Europe](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe") than in previous decades. All overt racial discrimination ended in 1973, with [multiculturalism](/wiki/Multiculturalism_in_Australia "Multiculturalism in Australia") becoming official policy.[[298]](#cite_note-304) Subsequently, there has been a large and continuing wave of immigration from across the world, with [Asia](/wiki/Asian_Australians "Asian Australians") being the largest source of immigrants in the 21st century.[[299]](#cite_note-Australian_Government_Department_of_Home_Affairs-2019-305)

Today, Australia has the world's [eighth-largest](/wiki/List_of_sovereign_states_and_dependent_territories_by_immigrant_population "List of sovereign states and dependent territories by immigrant population") immigrant population, with immigrants accounting for 30% of the population, the highest proportion among major [Western](/wiki/Western_world "Western world") nations.[[300]](#cite_note-306)[[301]](#cite_note-307) In 2022–23, 212,789 permanent migrants were admitted to Australia, with a net migration population gain of 518,000 people inclusive of non-permanent residents.[[302]](#cite_note-308)[[303]](#cite_note-309) Most entered on skilled visas,[[299]](#cite_note-Australian_Government_Department_of_Home_Affairs-2019-305) however the immigration program also offers visas for family members and [refugees](/wiki/Refugee "Refugee").[[304]](#cite_note-310)

The [Australian Bureau of Statistics](/wiki/Australian_Bureau_of_Statistics "Australian Bureau of Statistics") asks each Australian resident to nominate up to two [ancestries](/wiki/Ancestry "Ancestry") each [census](/wiki/Census_in_Australia "Census in Australia") and the responses are classified into broad ancestry groups.[[305]](#cite_note-Australian_Bureau_of_Statistics-2022-311)[[306]](#cite_note-Australian_Bureau_of_Statistics-2019-312) At the 2021 census, the most commonly nominated ancestry groups as a proportion of the total population were:[[307]](#cite_note-313) 57.2% [European](/wiki/European_Australians "European Australians") (including 46% [North-West European](/wiki/Northwestern_Europe#Ethnographic_definitions "Northwestern Europe") and 11.2% [Southern](/wiki/Southern_Europe "Southern Europe") and [Eastern European](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe")), 33.8% [Oceanian](/wiki/Demographics_of_Oceania "Demographics of Oceania"),[[N 7]](#cite_note-315) 17.4% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_Australians "Asian Australians") (including 6.5% [Southern](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia") and [Central Asian](/wiki/Central_Asia#Demographics "Central Asia"), 6.4% [North-East Asian](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia"), and 4.5% [South-East Asian](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia")), 3.2% [North African and Middle Eastern](/wiki/Demographics_of_the_Middle_East_and_North_Africa "Demographics of the Middle East and North Africa"), 1.4% [Peoples of the Americas](/wiki/Americas#Demography "Americas"), and 1.3% [Sub-Saharan African](/wiki/Sub-Saharan_Africa#Demographics "Sub-Saharan Africa"). At the 2021 census, the most commonly nominated individual ancestries as a proportion of the total population were:[[N 8]](#cite_note-316)[[4]](#cite_note-Australian_Bureau_of_Statistics-2022a-6)

* [English](/wiki/English_Australian "English Australian") (33%)
* [Australian](/wiki/Australians "Australians") (29.9%)[[N 9]](#cite_note-317)
* [Irish](/wiki/Irish_Australian "Irish Australian") (9.5%)
* [Scottish](/wiki/Scottish_Australian "Scottish Australian") (8.6%)
* [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_Australian "Chinese Australian") (5.5%)
* [Italian](/wiki/Italian_Australian "Italian Australian") (4.4%)
* [German](/wiki/German_Australian "German Australian") (4%)
* [Indian](/wiki/Indian_Australian "Indian Australian") (3.1%)
* [Aboriginal](/wiki/Aboriginal_Australians "Aboriginal Australians") (2.9%)[[N 10]](#cite_note-318)
* [Greek](/wiki/Greek_Australian "Greek Australian") (1.7%)
* [Filipino](/wiki/Filipino_Australian "Filipino Australian") (1.6%)
* [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_Australian "Dutch Australian") (1.5%)
* [Vietnamese](/wiki/Vietnamese_Australian "Vietnamese Australian") (1.3%)
* [Lebanese](/wiki/Lebanese_Australian "Lebanese Australian") (1%)

At the 2021 census, 3.8% of the Australian population identified as being [Indigenous](/wiki/Indigenous_Australians "Indigenous Australians") – [Aboriginal Australians](/wiki/Aboriginal_Australians "Aboriginal Australians") and [Torres Strait Islanders](/wiki/Torres_Strait_Islanders "Torres Strait Islanders").[[N 11]](#cite_note-319)[[306]](#cite_note-Australian_Bureau_of_Statistics-2019-312)

### Language

Main article: [Languages of Australia](/wiki/Languages_of_Australia "Languages of Australia")

English has no legal status in Australia but it is the *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")* official and national language due to its widespread established use.[[309]](#cite_note-320)[[310]](#cite_note-321) [Australian English](/wiki/Australian_English "Australian English") is a major variety of the language with a distinctive accent and lexicon,[[311]](#cite_note-322) and differs slightly from other varieties of English in grammar and spelling.[[312]](#cite_note-323) [General Australian](/wiki/General_Australian "General Australian") serves as the standard dialect.[[313]](#cite_note-324) The Australian [sign language](/wiki/Sign_language "Sign language") known as [Auslan](/wiki/Auslan "Auslan") was used at home by 16,242 people at the time of the 2021 census.[[314]](#cite_note-325)

At the 2021 census, English was the only language spoken in the home for 72% of the population. The next most common languages spoken at home were [Mandarin](/wiki/Mandarin_Chinese "Mandarin Chinese") (2.7%), [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language") (1.4%), [Vietnamese](/wiki/Vietnamese_language "Vietnamese language") (1.3%), [Cantonese](/wiki/Cantonese "Cantonese") (1.2%) and [Punjabi](/wiki/Punjabi_language "Punjabi language") (0.9%).[[315]](#cite_note-326)

More than 250 [Australian Aboriginal languages](/wiki/Australian_Aboriginal_languages "Australian Aboriginal languages") are thought to have existed at the time of first European contact.[[316]](#cite_note-327) The National Indigenous Languages Survey (NILS) for 2018–19 found that more than 120 Indigenous language varieties were in use or being revived, although 70 of those in use were endangered.[[317]](#cite_note-328) The 2021 census found that 167 Indigenous languages were spoken at home by 76,978 Indigenous Australians – Yumplatok ([Torres Strait Creole](/wiki/Torres_Strait_Creole "Torres Strait Creole")), [Djambarrpuyngu](/wiki/Djambarrpuyngu "Djambarrpuyngu") (a [Yolŋu language](/wiki/Yol%C5%8Bu_languages "Yolŋu languages")) and [Pitjantjatjara](/wiki/Pitjantjatjara_dialect "Pitjantjatjara dialect") (a [Western Desert language](/wiki/Western_Desert_language "Western Desert language")) were among the most widely spoken.[[318]](#cite_note-329) NILS and the Australian Bureau of Statistics use different classifications for Indigenous Australian languages.[[319]](#cite_note-330)

### Religion

Main article: [Religion in Australia](/wiki/Religion_in_Australia "Religion in Australia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/St_Mary%27s_Cathedral_as_viewed_from_Hyde_Park%2C_Sydney_b.jpg/250px-St_Mary%27s_Cathedral_as_viewed_from_Hyde_Park%2C_Sydney_b.jpg)](/wiki/File:St_Mary%27s_Cathedral_as_viewed_from_Hyde_Park,_Sydney_b.jpg)

[St Mary's Cathedral](/wiki/St_Mary%27s_Cathedral,_Sydney "St Mary's Cathedral, Sydney") in Sydney belongs to the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), Australia's largest religious denomination.

Australia has no [state religion](/wiki/State_religion "State religion"); section 116 of the [Australian Constitution](/wiki/Australian_Constitution "Australian Constitution") prohibits federal legislation
that would establish any religion, impose any religious observance, or prohibit the free exercise of any religion.[[320]](#cite_note-331) However, the states still retain the power to pass religiously discriminatory laws.[[321]](#cite_note-332)

At the 2021 census, 38.9% of the population identified as having [no religion](/wiki/Irreligion_in_Australia "Irreligion in Australia"),[[4]](#cite_note-Australian_Bureau_of_Statistics-2022a-6) up from 15.5% in 2001.[[322]](#cite_note-333) The largest religion is [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") (43.9% of the population).[[4]](#cite_note-Australian_Bureau_of_Statistics-2022a-6) The largest Christian denominations are the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_Australia "Catholic Church in Australia") (20% of the population) and the [Anglican Church of Australia](/wiki/Anglican_Church_of_Australia "Anglican Church of Australia") (9.8%). Non-British immigration since the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War") has led to the growth of non-Christian religions, the largest of which are [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") (3.2%), [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") (2.7%), [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") (2.4%), [Sikhism](/wiki/Sikhism "Sikhism") (0.8%), and [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism") (0.4%).[[323]](#cite_note-334)[[4]](#cite_note-Australian_Bureau_of_Statistics-2022a-6)

In 2021, just under 8,000 people declared an affiliation with traditional Aboriginal religions.[[4]](#cite_note-Australian_Bureau_of_Statistics-2022a-6) In [Australian Aboriginal mythology](/wiki/Australian_Aboriginal_mythology "Australian Aboriginal mythology") and the [animist](/wiki/Animist "Animist") framework developed in Aboriginal Australia, the [Dreaming](/wiki/Dreaming_(spirituality) "Dreaming (spirituality)") is a [sacred](/wiki/Sacred "Sacred") era in which ancestral [totemic](/wiki/Totem "Totem") spirit beings formed [The Creation](/wiki/Creation_myth "Creation myth"). The Dreaming established the laws and structures of society and the ceremonies performed to ensure continuity of life and land.[[324]](#cite_note-335)

### Health

See also: [Health care in Australia](/wiki/Health_care_in_Australia "Health care in Australia")

Australia's life expectancy of 83 years (81 years for males and 85 years for females)[[325]](#cite_note-336) is the [fifth-highest in the world](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_life_expectancy "List of countries by life expectancy"). It has the highest rate of skin cancer in the world,[[326]](#cite_note-337) while [cigarette smoking](/wiki/Tobacco_smoking "Tobacco smoking") is the largest preventable cause of death and disease, responsible for 7.8% of the total mortality and disease. Ranked second in preventable causes is [hypertension](/wiki/Hypertension "Hypertension") at 7.6%, with obesity third at 7.5%.[[327]](#cite_note-338)[[328]](#cite_note-339) Australia ranked 35th in the world in 2012 for its proportion of obese women[[329]](#cite_note-340) and near the top of [developed nations](/wiki/Developed_country "Developed country") for its proportion of [obese](/wiki/Obesity_in_Australia "Obesity in Australia") adults;[[330]](#cite_note-341) 63% of its adult population is either overweight or obese.[[331]](#cite_note-342)

Australia spent around 9.91% of its total GDP to health care in 2021.[[332]](#cite_note-343) It introduced a [national insurance scheme](/wiki/National_health_insurance "National health insurance") in 1975.[[333]](#cite_note-Biggs-2004-344) Following a period in which access to the scheme was restricted, the scheme became [universal](/wiki/Universal_health_care "Universal health care") once more in 1981 under the name of [Medicare](/wiki/Medicare_(Australia) "Medicare (Australia)").[[334]](#cite_note-345) The program is nominally funded by an income tax surcharge known as the [Medicare levy](/wiki/Medicare_levy "Medicare levy"), currently at 2%.[[335]](#cite_note-346) The states manage hospitals and attached outpatient services, while the Commonwealth funds the [Pharmaceutical Benefits Scheme](/wiki/Pharmaceutical_Benefits_Scheme "Pharmaceutical Benefits Scheme") (subsidising the costs of medicines) and [general practice](/wiki/General_practice "General practice").[[333]](#cite_note-Biggs-2004-344)

### Education

Main article: [Education in Australia](/wiki/Education_in_Australia "Education in Australia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Parkville_-_University_of_Melbourne_%28Ormond_College%29.jpg/250px-Parkville_-_University_of_Melbourne_%28Ormond_College%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Parkville_-_University_of_Melbourne_(Ormond_College).jpg)

Australia has the highest ratio of international students per capita in the world, with Melbourne ranking fifth among the 2023 *[QS Best Student Cities](/wiki/QS_World_University_Rankings#QS_Best_Student_Cities "QS World University Rankings")* ([University of Melbourne](/wiki/University_of_Melbourne "University of Melbourne") pictured).

School attendance, or registration for [home schooling](/wiki/Homeschooling_in_Australia "Homeschooling in Australia"),[[336]](#cite_note-347) is [compulsory](/wiki/Compulsory_education "Compulsory education") throughout Australia. Education is primarily the responsibility of the individual states and territories; however, the Commonwealth has significant influence through funding agreements.[[337]](#cite_note-348) Since 2014, [a national curriculum](/wiki/Australian_Curriculum "Australian Curriculum") developed by the Commonwealth has been implemented by the states and territories.[[338]](#cite_note-349) Attendance rules vary between states, but in general children are required to attend school from the age of about 5 until about 16.[[339]](#cite_note-350)[[340]](#cite_note-351) In some states (Western Australia, Northern Territory and New South Wales), children aged 16–17 are required to either attend school or participate in vocational training, such as an [apprenticeship](/wiki/Apprenticeship "Apprenticeship").[[341]](#cite_note-352)[[342]](#cite_note-353)[[343]](#cite_note-354)[[344]](#cite_note-355) According to the 2022 [PISA](/wiki/PISA "PISA") evaluations, Australian 15-year-olds ranked ninth in the OECD for reading and science and tenth for maths. However, less than 60% of Australian students achieved the National Proficiency Standard – 51% in maths, 58% in science and 57% in reading.[[345]](#cite_note-356)[[346]](#cite_note-357)

Australia has an adult literacy rate that was estimated to be 99% in 2003.[[347]](#cite_note-358) However, a 2011–2012 report for the Australian Bureau of Statistics found that 44% of the population does not have high literary and numeracy competence levels, interpreted by others as suggesting that they do not have the "skills needed for everyday life".[[348]](#cite_note-359)[[349]](#cite_note-360)[[350]](#cite_note-361)

Australia has 37 government-funded universities and three private universities, as well as a number of other specialist institutions that provide approved courses at the higher education level.[[351]](#cite_note-362) The OECD places Australia among the most expensive nations to attend university.[[352]](#cite_note-363) There is a state-based system of vocational training, known as [TAFE](/wiki/Technical_and_further_education "Technical and further education"), and many trades conduct apprenticeships for training new tradespeople.[[353]](#cite_note-364) About 58% of Australians aged from 25 to 64 have vocational or tertiary qualifications[[354]](#cite_note-365) and the tertiary graduation rate of 49% is the highest among OECD countries. 38% of Australia's population has attained a higher education qualification, which is among the highest percentages in the world.[[355]](#cite_note-366)[[356]](#cite_note-367)[[357]](#cite_note-368)

Australia has the highest ratio of [international students](/wiki/International_students_in_Australia "International students in Australia") per head of population in the world by a large margin, with 812,000 international students enrolled in the nation's universities and vocational institutions in 2019.[[358]](#cite_note-369)[[359]](#cite_note-370) Accordingly, in 2019, international students represented on average 26.7% of the student bodies of Australian universities. International education therefore represents one of the country's largest exports and has a pronounced influence on the country's demographics, with a significant proportion of international students remaining in Australia after graduation on various skill and employment visas.[[360]](#cite_note-371) Education is Australia's third-largest export, after iron ore and coal, and contributed more than $28 billion to the economy in the 2016–17 financial year.[[N 12]](#cite_note-372)[[285]](#cite_note-Berthold-2021-291)

## Culture

Main article: [Culture of Australia](/wiki/Culture_of_Australia "Culture of Australia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Sydney_Australia._%2821339175489%29.jpg/250px-Sydney_Australia._%2821339175489%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sydney_Australia._(21339175489).jpg)

The [Sydney Opera House](/wiki/Sydney_Opera_House "Sydney Opera House") was completed in 1973 and declared a UNESCO [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") in 2007, making it the youngest building to have received the designation.[[361]](#cite_note-373)

Contemporary Australian culture is diverse[[362]](#cite_note-374) and reflects [the country's Indigenous traditions](/wiki/Australian_Aboriginal_culture "Australian Aboriginal culture"), [British](/wiki/British_culture "British culture") and [Irish heritage](/wiki/Irish_culture "Irish culture"), and post-1945 history of [multicultural immigration](/wiki/Multiculturalism_in_Australia "Multiculturalism in Australia").[[363]](#cite_note-375)[[364]](#cite_note-376) The [culture of the United States](/wiki/Culture_of_the_United_States "Culture of the United States") has also been influential.[[365]](#cite_note-377) The evolution of Australian culture since British colonisation has given rise to distinctive cultural traits.[[366]](#cite_note-378)[[367]](#cite_note-379)

Many Australians identify [egalitarianism](/wiki/Egalitarianism "Egalitarianism"), [mateship](/wiki/Mateship "Mateship"), irreverence and a lack of formality as part of their [national identity](/wiki/National_identity "National identity").[[368]](#cite_note-380)[[369]](#cite_note-381)[[370]](#cite_note-382) These find expression in [Australian slang](/wiki/Australian_slang "Australian slang"), as well as [Australian humour](/wiki/Australian_comedy "Australian comedy"), which is often characterised as dry, irreverent and ironic.[[371]](#cite_note-383)[[372]](#cite_note-384) New citizens and visa holders are required to commit to "Australian values", which are identified by the [Department of Home Affairs](/wiki/Department_of_Home_Affairs_(Australia) "Department of Home Affairs (Australia)") as including: a respect for the freedom of the individual; recognition of the rule of law; opposition to racial, gender and religious discrimination; and an understanding of the "[fair go](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/fair_go "wikt:fair go")", which is said to encompass the equality of opportunity for all and compassion for those in need.[[373]](#cite_note-385) What these values mean, and whether Australians uphold them, has been debated since before Federation.[[374]](#cite_note-386)[[375]](#cite_note-387)[[376]](#cite_note-388)[[377]](#cite_note-389)

### Arts

Main articles: [Australian art](/wiki/Australian_art "Australian art"), [Australian literature](/wiki/Australian_literature "Australian literature"), [Theatre of Australia](/wiki/Theatre_of_Australia "Theatre of Australia"), [Dance in Australia](/wiki/Dance_in_Australia "Dance in Australia"), and [Music of Australia](/wiki/Music_of_Australia "Music of Australia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/Sidney_Nolan_Snake.jpg/250px-Sidney_Nolan_Snake.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sidney_Nolan_Snake.jpg)

Held at the [Museum of Old and New Art](/wiki/Museum_of_Old_and_New_Art "Museum of Old and New Art") in Hobart, Tasmania, [Sidney Nolan](/wiki/Sidney_Nolan "Sidney Nolan")'s *[Snake](/wiki/Snake_(Nolan) "Snake (Nolan)")* mural (1970) is inspired by the Aboriginal creation myth of the [Rainbow Serpent](/wiki/Rainbow_Serpent "Rainbow Serpent"), as well as desert flowers in bloom after a drought.[[378]](#cite_note-390)

Australia has more than 100,000 [Aboriginal rock art](/wiki/Indigenous_Australian_art#Stone_art "Indigenous Australian art") sites,[[379]](#cite_note-391) and traditional designs, patterns and stories infuse [contemporary Indigenous Australian art](/wiki/Contemporary_Indigenous_Australian_art "Contemporary Indigenous Australian art"), "the last great art movement of the 20th century" according to critic [Robert Hughes](/wiki/Robert_Hughes_(critic) "Robert Hughes (critic)");[[380]](#cite_note-392) its exponents include [Emily Kame Kngwarreye](/wiki/Emily_Kame_Kngwarreye "Emily Kame Kngwarreye").[[381]](#cite_note-393) Early colonial artists showed a fascination with the unfamiliar land.[[382]](#cite_note-www.artgallery.nsw.gov.au-394) The [impressionistic](/wiki/Impressionism "Impressionism") works of [Arthur Streeton](/wiki/Arthur_Streeton "Arthur Streeton"), [Tom Roberts](/wiki/Tom_Roberts "Tom Roberts") and other members of the 19th-century [Heidelberg School](/wiki/Heidelberg_School "Heidelberg School") – the first "distinctively Australian" movement in Western art – gave expression to nationalist sentiments in the lead-up to Federation.[[382]](#cite_note-www.artgallery.nsw.gov.au-394) While the school remained influential into the 1900s, [modernists](/wiki/Modern_art "Modern art") such as [Margaret Preston](/wiki/Margaret_Preston "Margaret Preston") and [Clarice Beckett](/wiki/Clarice_Beckett "Clarice Beckett"), and, later, [Sidney Nolan](/wiki/Sidney_Nolan "Sidney Nolan"), explored new artistic trends.[[382]](#cite_note-www.artgallery.nsw.gov.au-394) The landscape remained central to the work of Aboriginal watercolourist [Albert Namatjira](/wiki/Albert_Namatjira "Albert Namatjira"),[[383]](#cite_note-395) as well as [Fred Williams](/wiki/Fred_Williams_(artist) "Fred Williams (artist)"), [Brett Whiteley](/wiki/Brett_Whiteley "Brett Whiteley") and other post-war artists whose works, eclectic in style yet uniquely Australian, moved between the [figurative](/wiki/Figurative_art "Figurative art") and the [abstract](/wiki/Abstract_art "Abstract art").[[382]](#cite_note-www.artgallery.nsw.gov.au-394)[[384]](#cite_note-396)

Australian literature grew slowly in the decades following European settlement though Indigenous [oral traditions](/wiki/Oral_tradition "Oral tradition"), many of which have since been recorded in writing, are much older.[[385]](#cite_note-397) In the 19th century, [Henry Lawson](/wiki/Henry_Lawson "Henry Lawson") and [Banjo Paterson](/wiki/Banjo_Paterson "Banjo Paterson") captured the experience of [the bush](/wiki/Australian_bush "Australian bush") using a distinctive Australian vocabulary.[[386]](#cite_note-398) Their works are still popular; Paterson's [bush poem](/wiki/Bush_poetry "Bush poetry") "[Waltzing Matilda](/wiki/Waltzing_Matilda "Waltzing Matilda")" (1895) is regarded as Australia's unofficial national anthem.[[387]](#cite_note-399) [Miles Franklin](/wiki/Miles_Franklin "Miles Franklin") is the namesake of Australia's [most prestigious literary prize](/wiki/Miles_Franklin_Award "Miles Franklin Award"), awarded annually to the best novel about Australian life.[[388]](#cite_note-400) Its first recipient, [Patrick White](/wiki/Patrick_White "Patrick White"), went on to win the [Nobel Prize in Literature](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Literature "Nobel Prize in Literature") in 1973.[[389]](#cite_note-401) Australian [Booker Prize](/wiki/Booker_Prize "Booker Prize") winners include [Peter Carey](/wiki/Peter_Carey_(novelist) "Peter Carey (novelist)"), [Thomas Keneally](/wiki/Thomas_Keneally "Thomas Keneally") and [Richard Flanagan](/wiki/Richard_Flanagan "Richard Flanagan").[[390]](#cite_note-402) Australian public intellectuals have also written seminal works in their respective fields, including feminist [Germaine Greer](/wiki/Germaine_Greer "Germaine Greer") and philosopher [Peter Singer](/wiki/Peter_Singer "Peter Singer").[[391]](#cite_note-403)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/ACDC-Hughes-long_ago.jpg/250px-ACDC-Hughes-long_ago.jpg)](/wiki/File:ACDC-Hughes-long_ago.jpg)

Arising from the [Australian pub rock](/wiki/Pub_rock_(Australia) "Pub rock (Australia)") scene, [AC/DC](/wiki/AC/DC "AC/DC") ranks among the world's [best-selling music acts](/wiki/List_of_best-selling_music_artists "List of best-selling music artists").

In the performing arts, Aboriginal peoples have traditions of religious and secular song, dance and rhythmic music often performed in [corroborees](/wiki/Corroboree "Corroboree").[[46]](#cite_note-Flood_2019-52) At the beginning of the 20th century, [Nellie Melba](/wiki/Nellie_Melba "Nellie Melba") was one of the world's leading opera singers,[[392]](#cite_note-404) and later popular music acts such as the [Bee Gees](/wiki/Bee_Gees "Bee Gees"), [AC/DC](/wiki/AC/DC "AC/DC"), [INXS](/wiki/INXS "INXS") and [Kylie Minogue](/wiki/Kylie_Minogue "Kylie Minogue") achieved international recognition.[[393]](#cite_note-405) Many of Australia's performing arts companies receive funding through the Australian government's [Australia Council](/wiki/Australia_Council_for_the_Arts "Australia Council for the Arts").[[394]](#cite_note-406) There is a symphony orchestra in each state,[[395]](#cite_note-407) and a national opera company, [Opera Australia](/wiki/Opera_Australia "Opera Australia"),[[396]](#cite_note-408) well known for its famous [soprano](/wiki/Soprano "Soprano") [Joan Sutherland](/wiki/Joan_Sutherland "Joan Sutherland").[[397]](#cite_note-409) Ballet and dance are represented by [The Australian Ballet](/wiki/The_Australian_Ballet "The Australian Ballet") and various state companies. Each state has a publicly funded theatre company.[[398]](#cite_note-410)

### Media

Main articles: [Cinema of Australia](/wiki/Cinema_of_Australia "Cinema of Australia"), [Television in Australia](/wiki/Television_in_Australia "Television in Australia"), and [Media of Australia](/wiki/Media_of_Australia "Media of Australia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/The_Story_of_the_Kelly_Gang_1906.jpg/250px-The_Story_of_the_Kelly_Gang_1906.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Story_of_the_Kelly_Gang_1906.jpg)

Actor playing the [bushranger](/wiki/Bushranger "Bushranger") and outlaw [Ned Kelly](/wiki/Ned_Kelly "Ned Kelly") in *[The Story of the Kelly Gang](/wiki/The_Story_of_the_Kelly_Gang "The Story of the Kelly Gang")* (1906), the world's first feature-length narrative film

*[The Story of the Kelly Gang](/wiki/The_Story_of_the_Kelly_Gang "The Story of the Kelly Gang")* (1906), the world's first [feature-length](/wiki/Feature_film "Feature film") narrative film, spurred a boom in Australian cinema during the [silent film](/wiki/Silent_film "Silent film") era.[[399]](#cite_note-411) After World War I, [Hollywood](/wiki/Hollywood_(film_industry) "Hollywood (film industry)") monopolised the industry,[[400]](#cite_note-412) and by the 1960s, Australian film production had effectively ceased.[[401]](#cite_note-413) With the benefit of government support, the [Australian New Wave](/wiki/Australian_New_Wave "Australian New Wave") of the 1970s brought provocative and successful films, many exploring themes of national identity, such as *[Picnic at Hanging Rock](/wiki/Picnic_at_Hanging_Rock_(film) "Picnic at Hanging Rock (film)")*, *[Wake in Fright](/wiki/Wake_in_Fright "Wake in Fright")* and *[Gallipoli](/wiki/Gallipoli_(1981_film) "Gallipoli (1981 film)")*,[[402]](#cite_note-414) while *[Crocodile Dundee](/wiki/Crocodile_Dundee "Crocodile Dundee")* and the [Ozploitation](/wiki/Ozploitation "Ozploitation") movement's *[Mad Max](/wiki/Mad_Max_(franchise) "Mad Max (franchise)")* series became international blockbusters.[[403]](#cite_note-415) In a film market flooded with foreign content, Australian films delivered a 7.7% share of the local box office in 2015.[[404]](#cite_note-416) The [AACTAs](/wiki/AACTA_Awards "AACTA Awards") are Australia's premier film and television awards, and notable [Academy Award winners from Australia](/wiki/List_of_Australian_Academy_Award_winners_and_nominees "List of Australian Academy Award winners and nominees") include [Geoffrey Rush](/wiki/Geoffrey_Rush "Geoffrey Rush"), [Nicole Kidman](/wiki/Nicole_Kidman "Nicole Kidman"), [Cate Blanchett](/wiki/Cate_Blanchett "Cate Blanchett") and [Heath Ledger](/wiki/Heath_Ledger "Heath Ledger").[[405]](#cite_note-417)

Australia has two public broadcasters (the [Australian Broadcasting Corporation](/wiki/Australian_Broadcasting_Corporation "Australian Broadcasting Corporation") and the multicultural [Special Broadcasting Service](/wiki/Special_Broadcasting_Service "Special Broadcasting Service")), three commercial television networks, several pay-TV services,[[406]](#cite_note-Country_profile-2009-418) and numerous public, non-profit television and radio stations. Each major city has at least one daily newspaper,[[406]](#cite_note-Country_profile-2009-418) and there are two national daily newspapers, *[The Australian](/wiki/The_Australian "The Australian")* and *[The Australian Financial Review](/wiki/The_Australian_Financial_Review "The Australian Financial Review")*.[[406]](#cite_note-Country_profile-2009-418) In 2024, [Reporters Without Borders](/wiki/Reporters_Without_Borders "Reporters Without Borders") placed Australia 39th on a list of 180 countries ranked by [press freedom](/wiki/Freedom_of_the_press "Freedom of the press"), behind New Zealand (19th) and the United Kingdom (23rd), but ahead of the United States (55th).[[407]](#cite_note-419) This relatively low ranking is primarily because of the limited diversity of commercial media ownership in Australia;[[408]](#cite_note-420) most print media are under the control of [News Corp Australia](/wiki/News_Corp_Australia "News Corp Australia") (59%) and [Nine Entertainment](/wiki/Nine_Entertainment "Nine Entertainment") (23%).[[409]](#cite_note-421)

### Cuisine

Main article: [Australian cuisine](/wiki/Australian_cuisine "Australian cuisine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Shiraz_Wein.jpg/250px-Shiraz_Wein.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shiraz_Wein.jpg)

[South Australian wines](/wiki/South_Australian_wine "South Australian wine")

Most Indigenous Australian groups subsisted on a diet of native fauna and flora, otherwise called [bush tucker](/wiki/Bush_tucker "Bush tucker").[[410]](#cite_note-422) It has increased in popularity among non-Indigenous Australians since the 1970s, with examples such as [lemon myrtle](/wiki/Backhousia_citriodora "Backhousia citriodora"), the [macadamia nut](/wiki/Macadamia "Macadamia") and [kangaroo meat](/wiki/Kangaroo_meat "Kangaroo meat") now widely available.[[411]](#cite_note-423)[[412]](#cite_note-424)

The first colonists introduced [British](/wiki/British_cuisine "British cuisine") and [Irish cuisine](/wiki/Irish_cuisine "Irish cuisine") to the continent.[[413]](#cite_note-425)[[414]](#cite_note-426) This influence is seen in dishes such as [fish and chips](/wiki/Fish_and_chips "Fish and chips"), and in the [Australian meat pie](/wiki/Meat_pie_(Australia_and_New_Zealand) "Meat pie (Australia and New Zealand)"), which is related to the British [steak pie](/wiki/Steak_pie "Steak pie"). Also during the colonial period, Chinese migrants paved the way for a distinctive [Australian Chinese cuisine](/wiki/Australian_Chinese_cuisine "Australian Chinese cuisine").[[415]](#cite_note-427)

Post-war migrants transformed Australian cuisine, bringing with them their culinary traditions and contributing to new [fusion](/wiki/Fusion_cuisine "Fusion cuisine") dishes.[[416]](#cite_note-428) Italians introduced espresso coffee and, along with Greeks, helped develop Australia's café culture, of which the [flat white](/wiki/Flat_white "Flat white") and [avocado toast](/wiki/Avocado_toast "Avocado toast") are now considered Australian staples.[[417]](#cite_note-429)[[418]](#cite_note-430) [Pavlovas](/wiki/Pavlova_(food) "Pavlova (food)"), [lamingtons](/wiki/Lamington "Lamington"), [Vegemite](/wiki/Vegemite "Vegemite") and [Anzac biscuits](/wiki/Anzac_biscuits "Anzac biscuits") are also often called iconic Australian foods.[[419]](#cite_note-431)

Australia is a leading exporter and consumer of [wine](/wiki/Wine "Wine").[[420]](#cite_note-432) [Australian wine](/wiki/Australian_wine "Australian wine") is produced mainly in the southern, cooler parts of the country.[[421]](#cite_note-433) The nation also ranks highly in [beer consumption](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_beer_consumption_per_capita "List of countries by beer consumption per capita"),[[422]](#cite_note-434) with each state and territory hosting numerous breweries.

### Sport and recreation

Main article: [Sport in Australia](/wiki/Sport_in_Australia "Sport in Australia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/2017_AFL_Grand_Final_panorama_during_national_anthem.jpg/250px-2017_AFL_Grand_Final_panorama_during_national_anthem.jpg)](/wiki/File:2017_AFL_Grand_Final_panorama_during_national_anthem.jpg)

The [Melbourne Cricket Ground](/wiki/Melbourne_Cricket_Ground "Melbourne Cricket Ground") is strongly associated with the history and development of [cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket") and [Australian rules football](/wiki/Australian_rules_football "Australian rules football"), Australia's two most popular spectator sports.[[423]](#cite_note-435)

The most popular sports in Australia by adult participation are: swimming, athletics, cycling, soccer, golf, tennis, basketball, surfing, netball and cricket.[[424]](#cite_note-436)

Australia is one of five nations to have participated in every [Summer Olympics](/wiki/Summer_Olympics "Summer Olympics") of the modern era,[[425]](#cite_note-437) and has hosted the Games twice: [1956](/wiki/1956_Summer_Olympics "1956 Summer Olympics") in Melbourne and [2000](/wiki/2000_Summer_Olympics "2000 Summer Olympics") in Sydney.[[426]](#cite_note-438) It is also set to host the [2032 Games](/wiki/2032_Summer_Olympics "2032 Summer Olympics") in [Brisbane](/wiki/Brisbane "Brisbane").[[427]](#cite_note-439) Australia has also participated in every [Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games "Commonwealth Games"),[[428]](#cite_note-440) hosting the event in [1938](/wiki/1938_British_Empire_Games "1938 British Empire Games"), [1962](/wiki/1962_British_Empire_and_Commonwealth_Games "1962 British Empire and Commonwealth Games"), [1982](/wiki/1982_Commonwealth_Games "1982 Commonwealth Games"), [2006](/wiki/2006_Commonwealth_Games "2006 Commonwealth Games") and [2018](/wiki/2018_Commonwealth_Games "2018 Commonwealth Games").[[429]](#cite_note-441)

The [Australian national cricket team](/wiki/Australia_national_cricket_team "Australia national cricket team") competed against [England](/wiki/England_cricket_team "England cricket team") in both the first [Test](/wiki/Test_cricket "Test cricket") match (1877) and the first [One Day International](/wiki/One_Day_International "One Day International") (1971), and against [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand_cricket_team "New Zealand cricket team") in the first [Twenty20 International](/wiki/Twenty20_International "Twenty20 International") (2004), winning all three games.[[430]](#cite_note-442) It has also won the men's [Cricket World Cup](/wiki/Cricket_World_Cup "Cricket World Cup") a record six times.[[431]](#cite_note-443)

Australia has professional leagues for [four football codes](/wiki/Football_in_Australia "Football in Australia"), whose relative popularity is [divided geographically](/wiki/Barassi_Line "Barassi Line").[[432]](#cite_note-444) Originating in Melbourne in the 1850s, [Australian rules football](/wiki/Australian_rules_football "Australian rules football") attracts the most television viewers in all states except New South Wales and Queensland, where [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league") holds sway, followed by [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union").[[433]](#cite_note-445) [Soccer](/wiki/Association_football "Association football"), while ranked fourth in television viewers and resources, has the highest overall participation rates.[[434]](#cite_note-446)

The [surf lifesaving](/wiki/Surf_lifesaving "Surf lifesaving") movement originated in Australia in the early 20th century, following the relaxation of laws prohibiting daylight bathing on Australian beaches. The volunteer lifesaver is one of the country's icons.[[435]](#cite_note-447)[[436]](#cite_note-448)

## Notes

1. **[^](#cite_ref-anthem_explanation_2-0)** Australia also has a [royal anthem](/wiki/Royal_anthem "Royal anthem"), "[God Save the King](/wiki/God_Save_the_King "God Save the King")", which may be played in place of or alongside the national anthem when members of the [royal family](/wiki/House_of_Windsor "House of Windsor") are present. If not played alongside the royal anthem, the national anthem is instead played at the end of an official event.[[1]](#cite_note-1)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-5)** Sydney is the largest city based on Australian Bureau of Statistics (ABS) Greater Capital City Statistical Areas (GCCSAs). These represent labour markets and the functional area of Australian capital cities.[[2]](#cite_note-3) Melbourne is larger based on ABS Significant Urban Areas (SUAs). These represent Urban Centres, or groups of contiguous Urban Centres, that contain a population of 10,000 people or more.[[3]](#cite_note-4)
3. **[^](#cite_ref-7)** The religion question is optional in the Australian census.
4. **[^](#cite_ref-8)** Pronounced "Ozzy"
5. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-time_19-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-time_19-1) There are minor variations from three basic time zones; see [Time in Australia](/wiki/Time_in_Australia "Time in Australia").
6. **[^](#cite_ref-22)** [42% of the Antarctic continent is also claimed by the country](/wiki/Australian_Antarctic_Territory "Australian Antarctic Territory"); however this is only recognised by the UK, France, New Zealand and Norway.[[16]](#cite_note-Scott_2021-21)
7. **[^](#cite_ref-315)** Includes those who nominate "Australian" as their ancestry.[[4]](#cite_note-Australian_Bureau_of_Statistics-2022a-6) The Australian Bureau of Statistics has stated that most who nominate "Australian" as their ancestry have at least partial [Anglo-Celtic](/wiki/Anglo-Celtic_Australian "Anglo-Celtic Australian") [European](/wiki/European_Australian "European Australian") ancestry.[[308]](#cite_note-1301.0_–_Year_Book_Australia,_1995-314)
8. **[^](#cite_ref-316)** Each person may nominate more than one ancestry, so the total may exceed 100%.[[305]](#cite_note-Australian_Bureau_of_Statistics-2022-311)
9. **[^](#cite_ref-317)** The Australian Bureau of Statistics has stated that most who nominate "Australian" as their ancestry have at least partial [Anglo-Celtic](/wiki/Anglo-Celtic_Australian "Anglo-Celtic Australian") [European](/wiki/European_Australian "European Australian") ancestry.[[308]](#cite_note-1301.0_–_Year_Book_Australia,_1995-314)
10. **[^](#cite_ref-318)** Those who nominated their ancestry as "Australian Aboriginal". Does not include [Torres Strait Islanders](/wiki/Torres_Strait_Islanders "Torres Strait Islanders"). This relates to nomination of ancestry and is distinct from persons who identify as Indigenous (Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander), which is a separate question.
11. **[^](#cite_ref-319)** Indigenous identification is separate to the ancestry question on the Australian Census and persons identifying as Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander may identify any ancestry.
12. **[^](#cite_ref-372)** That is, 1 July 2016 to 30 June 2017.
